The thresholds are configured in the monitoring system (Prometheus alerting rules or Grafana alert conditions), not in the pipeline code — so they can be tuned without redeploying the pipeline. At CelticWind, thresholds are reviewed quarterly alongside the performance review (Module 8, lesson 9): if the baseline shifts (a new sub-region increases the normal rejection rate from 0.04% to 0.06%), the threshold is adjusted to avoid false positives.
Preventing Alert Fatigue

Alert fatigue occurs when alerts fire frequently without requiring action — the team learns to ignore them, and eventually misses a real incident. CelticWind prevents alert fatigue with three policies. First, every alert must have a runbook: if the on-call engineer cannot follow a documented procedure to investigate and resolve the alert, the alert should not exist. Second, alerts that fire more than twice per week without leading to a remediation action are reviewed and either tuned (threshold too sensitive) or removed (the metric does not indicate an actionable problem). Third, alerts use a 5-minute evaluation window: the metric must exceed the threshold for 5 consecutive minutes before the alert fires, filtering out transient spikes that resolve without intervention.

blocks.title: An alert without a runbook is just noise
blocks.content: When the on-call engineer receives an alert at 03:15, they need to know three things: what might be wrong (the rejection rate exceeded 1%), what to check first (look at the rejection breakdown by field in the validation report), and what to do (if the problem is a source data change, contact the source team; if it is a validation bug, roll back to the previous version). The runbook provides these answers. At CelticWind, every alert in PagerDuty links to its runbook page in the team wiki. An alert without a runbook link is automatically escalated to the platform team for review.

Metrics answer specific questions: is the rejection rate above 1%? Is the duration exceeding 80% of the SLA? Dashboards organize metrics into visual narratives that answer broader operational questions: is the platform healthy right now (the overview dashboard), what happened during a specific pipeline run (the detail dashboard), and how are trends evolving over weeks and months (the historical dashboard)? At CelticWind, three Grafana dashboards serve different audiences: the overview dashboard for the morning standup, the detail dashboard for incident investigation, and the historical dashboard for the quarterly performance review.
The Three Dashboards

rows: All 12 pipelines: last run status (green/red), duration, rows processed, rejection rate. Single-glance health summary.
Per-step timing breakdown, rejection breakdown by field, memory usage, I/O timings, log stream (last 50 events)
Duration trend (30 days), rejection rate trend (30 days), data volume growth, SLA headroom projection

blocks.content: The overview dashboard answers 'is everything okay?' in under 10 seconds: a grid of 12 pipeline tiles, each colored green (last run succeeded, within SLA) or red (last run failed or exceeded SLA). The detail dashboard answers 'what went wrong?' during an incident: the engineer clicks the red tile and sees the step-by-step timing, the validation breakdown, and the last 50 log events for that run. The historical dashboard answers 'where are we heading?' during the quarterly review: duration trends show whether pipelines are approaching their SLAs, volume trends show data growth, and the SLA headroom projection estimates when each pipeline will breach its SLA at current growth rates.
Dashboard Design Principles

Three principles guide CelticWind's dashboard design. First, the overview dashboard must be glanceable: no scrolling, no clicking, no reading — the health of all 12 pipelines is visible in a single screen. Second, the detail dashboard must be drillable: from the overview tile, one click reaches the per-step breakdown, another click reaches the per-field rejection breakdown, and another click reaches the raw log events. Third, the historical dashboard must be actionable: every trend line has a reference line showing the SLA threshold, so the quarterly reviewer can immediately see which pipelines are approaching their limits without interpreting the numbers.

blocks.title: Design dashboards for the reader's question, not for the metrics available
blocks.content: The common mistake is creating a dashboard with every available metric and letting the reader figure out what matters. This produces a wall of charts that nobody reads. At CelticWind, each dashboard is designed around a specific question: 'Is the platform healthy?' (overview), 'What failed in this run?' (detail), 'When will we need to optimize?' (historical). Metrics that do not help answer the dashboard's question are not shown on that dashboard — even if they are available.

This module has built a substantial observability stack: structlog with JSON output and correlation IDs, Prometheus metrics with 5 core metrics and dimensional labels, health check endpoints for Kubernetes probes, alerting with runbook-linked thresholds, and three-tier dashboards. Building and maintaining this stack has a cost: the structlog configuration must be maintained across upgrades, Prometheus requires storage and a scraping server, dashboards must be updated when pipelines change, and alert thresholds must be reviewed quarterly. The question is: does every pipeline need the full stack?
The Observability Decision Framework

rows: structlog, Prometheus metrics, health checks, alerts, dashboards
Nothing — high-frequency pipelines need full visibility
structlog, Prometheus metrics, alerts for SLA and quality
Health check endpoints (the daily scheduler handles scheduling)
structlog, structured validation report, email notification on failure
Prometheus metrics (weekly data points are too sparse for meaningful trends)
structlog with JSON output for post-hoc analysis
Metrics, dashboards, alerts (the operator reviews logs manually after each run)

blocks.content: The framework scales observability investment to operational risk. A pipeline that runs every hour and feeds a real-time dashboard needs instant failure detection (health checks, sub-minute metrics, PagerDuty alerts). A pipeline that runs monthly and produces an internal report needs only logs and an email notification on failure — the operator reviews the output manually. At CelticWind, the 4 Bronze ingestion pipelines (daily) and 4 Silver transformation pipelines (daily) have the full observability stack. The 3 Gold aggregation pipelines (daily) have the standard stack (no health checks because they are downstream and triggered by the Silver pipeline's success). The monthly reporting pipeline has logs only.
The Operational Overhead of Monitoring

Monitoring infrastructure has its own failure modes: Prometheus runs out of storage and stops scraping, Grafana dashboards break after a Prometheus upgrade, alert rules reference metrics that were renamed, and the health check server consumes a port that conflicts with another service. Each component adds operational surface area that the platform team must maintain. At CelticWind, the observability stack is treated as infrastructure with its own SLA: Prometheus and Grafana are monitored by the cloud provider's built-in monitoring (Azure Monitor), and the health check server is covered by Kubernetes's own pod management. The team budgets 2 hours per week for observability maintenance: dashboard updates, threshold tuning, and alert rule hygiene.

blocks.title: Monitoring that nobody checks is worse than no monitoring
blocks.content: A dashboard that nobody opens provides false confidence: the team believes the pipeline is monitored when it is not. An alert that nobody responds to is worse than no alert: it trains the team to ignore the alerting system. At CelticWind, the weekly operations review checks two things: which alerts fired and how quickly they were acknowledged, and which dashboards were viewed during the week. Dashboards with zero views in a month are candidates for removal. Alerts with zero acknowledgments in a quarter are reviewed for relevance.

This module taught the three pillars of observability for data pipelines: structured logging with structlog for machine-parseable, context-rich log events (lessons 2-3), Prometheus metrics for quantitative measurement with dimensional labels and alertable thresholds (lessons 4-5), and health checks for pre-run environment validation and container lifecycle management (lesson 6). It also taught the operational discipline: alert threshold design with runbook links (lesson 7), dashboard organization for different audiences and questions (lesson 8), and the decision framework that scales observability to operational risk (lesson 9). This lesson presents CelticWind's production observability stack.
The Observability Architecture

blocks.title: CelticWind observability data flow
blocks.content:
Pipeline Process                  Observability Infrastructure
  ┌────────────────────┐     ┌────────────────────────────┐
  │ structlog           │───▶│ Log aggregator (CloudWatch)  │
  │ (JSON lines to      │     │ → Search by run_id           │
  │  stdout)             │     │ → Filter by event name       │
  └────────────────────┘     └────────────────────────────┘
  ┌────────────────────┐     ┌────────────────────────────┐
  │ prometheus-client    │───▶│ Prometheus (scrapes :9090)   │
  │ (:9090 /metrics)     │     │ → 5 core metrics + labels   │
  │                      │     │ → Alert rules → PagerDuty   │
  └────────────────────┘     └────────────┬───────────────┘
  ┌────────────────────┐              │
  │ Health checks       │     ┌──────┴─────────────────────┐
  │ (:8080 /healthz     │     │ Grafana Dashboards          │
  │        /readyz)     │     │ • Platform Overview (standup)│
  └────────────────────┘     │ • Pipeline Detail (incident) │
        │                       │ • Historical Trends (review) │
        └───▶ Kubernetes probes   └────────────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: The pipeline process exposes three observability surfaces: structlog JSON lines to stdout (consumed by the log aggregator), Prometheus metrics on port 9090 (scraped by Prometheus, visualized in Grafana, alerted via PagerDuty), and health check endpoints on port 8080 (probed by Kubernetes for liveness and readiness). The three surfaces together provide logs, metrics, and health checks — the complete observability stack.

blocks.content: The stack has clean separation of concerns: structlog handles the narrative (what happened during the run), Prometheus handles the numbers (how much happened, how fast, how often), and health checks handle the binary status (is the environment healthy). The pipeline code interacts with structlog (logger.info calls) and prometheus-client (counter.inc calls); the infrastructure handles collection, storage, visualization, and alerting. At CelticWind, the observability stack detected the Tuesday incident from lesson 1 within 15 seconds of the pipeline's completion: the rows_processed counter was 0 (below the 1,000 threshold), which triggered a Slack alert at 03:15 and a PagerDuty page at 03:20 — 4 hours before the billing team would have noticed.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 10 completes the course with production operations: idempotency guarantees (re-runnable without duplication), checkpointing for long-running pipelines, graceful shutdown on SIGTERM, incident response procedures, operational runbooks, data reprocessing strategies, SLA management, and capacity planning. The observability stack from this module provides the detection layer: alerts fire when something goes wrong. Module 10 provides the response layer: what to do when the alert fires, how to investigate, how to fix, and how to prevent recurrence. Together, they form the complete operational discipline for production data pipelines.

chapters.title: Production Operations
chapters.description: Operate production pipelines with idempotency guarantees, checkpointing, graceful shutdown, incident response, and the operational runbook that the on-call engineer follows.

blocks.content: The pipeline is deployed (Module 7), monitored (Module 9), and observable (structlog, Prometheus, health checks). It runs every night at 03:00, processes 1.2 million meter readings, and produces Silver Parquet output by 04:03. Then Friday happens. The EPEX SPOT API returns a 500 error at 03:02. The pipeline retries three times, exhausts the retry budget, and fails. The scheduler retries the entire pipeline at 03:30. The pipeline reads the same Bronze data, validates it again, transforms it again — but now the Silver output from the partial first run exists alongside the output from the second run. Monday morning, the billing team reports that Dublin's consumption is doubled. The pipeline ran twice and wrote twice: it was not idempotent.
The Four Operational Properties

rows: Running the pipeline twice on the same input produces the same output as running it once
Retries produce duplicate data. The billing team invoices customers twice.
A failed pipeline can restart from where it stopped, not from the beginning
A failure at step 7 of 10 requires reprocessing steps 1-6 (wasting 45 minutes of compute)
The pipeline can be stopped cleanly without corrupting output
A SIGTERM during a Parquet write produces a corrupt file that crashes downstream consumers
The on-call engineer can determine what went wrong from logs, metrics, and runbooks alone
Diagnosis requires SSH access to the production server, reading code, and guessing at the failure cause

blocks.content: Module 9 made the pipeline diagnosable (structlog, metrics, alerts). This module makes it idempotent (lesson 2), resumable (lesson 3), and stoppable (lesson 4). Together, the four properties transform a deployed pipeline into a production pipeline: one that the team can operate with confidence — retrying without fear of duplication, stopping without fear of corruption, and diagnosing without fear of ambiguity. These are the properties that let the on-call engineer sleep through the night, knowing that any failure is recoverable without manual intervention.

blocks.title: Operations is the longest phase of a pipeline's life
blocks.content: Building the pipeline takes weeks. Deploying it takes hours. Operating it takes years. The patterns in this module — idempotency, checkpointing, graceful shutdown, incident response, runbooks — determine whether those years are smooth or chaotic. A pipeline without operational discipline works on day one and degrades by day ninety: data drifts, retries produce duplicates, failures require heroic debugging sessions, and new team members cannot operate it without tribal knowledge. Operational discipline makes the pipeline's behavior predictable and its failures recoverable.

An idempotent pipeline produces the same output regardless of how many times it runs on the same input. If the 03:00 run fails and the 03:30 retry succeeds, the Silver output is identical to what a single successful 03:00 run would have produced — no duplicates, no missing records, no partial results. Idempotency is the foundation of reliable operations because retries are inevitable: APIs fail transiently, servers restart, network connections drop. Without idempotency, every retry is a gamble that may produce correct output, duplicate output, or a mixture of both.
The Overwrite Pattern for Silver

from pathlib import Path
import shutil
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def write_silver_idempotent(
    records: list[dict],
    silver_dir: Path,
    date: str,
) -> None:
    """Write Silver output idempotently using atomic overwrite.

    If a previous run wrote partial output for this date, it is replaced entirely.
    """
    partition_dir = silver_dir / f'date={date}'
    temp_dir = silver_dir / f'.tmp_date={date}'

    # Step 1: Write to a temporary directory
    temp_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    write_parquet(records, temp_dir / 'data.parquet')

    # Step 2: Atomically replace the partition directory
    if partition_dir.exists():
        shutil.rmtree(partition_dir)  # remove previous output
    temp_dir.rename(partition_dir)    # atomic rename on the same filesystem

    logger.info('Silver written idempotently: date=%s rows=%d', date, len(records))

# First run at 03:00 (fails after writing partial data):
#   .tmp_date=2025-01-15/data.parquet exists, date=2025-01-15/ does not
# Retry at 03:30 (succeeds):
#   .tmp_date=2025-01-15/ is written fresh, renamed to date=2025-01-15/
#   Result: exactly one complete output, no duplicates

The pattern writes to a temporary directory first and renames it to the final location only after the write completes successfully. If the pipeline fails during the write (the temporary directory contains partial data), the next retry writes a fresh temporary directory and renames it — overwriting any previous partial output. The rename operation (temp_dir.rename(partition_dir)) is atomic on the same filesystem: it either completes entirely or does not happen at all, preventing the output directory from being in a half-written state. At CelticWind, the Silver pipeline uses the overwrite pattern for every date partition: each run replaces the previous output for that date, making retries safe.
Idempotency for Gold Aggregations

The Silver layer uses full overwrite: recompute and replace the entire partition for a date. The Gold layer has two options. Full recompute: recalculate all Gold aggregations from the Silver data on every run. This is idempotent by definition (same input always produces same output) but expensive for large aggregations that span months. Incremental upsert: update only the rows affected by the current day's Silver data, using a MERGE-like pattern that inserts new rows and updates existing ones. At CelticWind, the daily Gold aggregation uses full recompute because the aggregation takes only 4 seconds with Polars (Module 3). The monthly Gold report uses incremental upsert because recomputing 30 days of aggregated data is wasteful when only 1 day changed. Both approaches are idempotent: retries produce the same result.

blocks.title: Append is never idempotent
blocks.content: A pipeline that appends rows to an existing file (write_parquet in append mode) produces duplicate rows when retried: the first run appends 1.2M rows, the retry appends another 1.2M rows, and the output has 2.4M rows. At CelticWind, the code review checklist includes: 'Does the write operation use overwrite or atomic replace? Append mode is not permitted for production pipelines.' The only exception is the quarantine log, which intentionally accumulates rejected records across runs for investigation — and even that is partitioned by date so that a retry overwrites the day's quarantine, not the entire history.

CelticWind's daily Silver pipeline completes in 63 seconds. A failure at any point requires at most 63 seconds to reprocess from the beginning. CelticWind's annual reprocessing job (recomputing 365 days of Silver output after a validation rule change) takes 8 hours. A failure at hour 7 — after successfully processing 320 of 365 days — would require restarting from day 1 and repeating 7 hours of work. Checkpointing saves progress periodically so that a failure resumes from the last checkpoint, not from the beginning. The annual job checkpoints after each day: a failure at day 320 resumes from day 321, saving 7 hours of redundant work.
A Simple File-Based Checkpoint

The CheckpointManager writes a JSON file containing the last successfully processed date. On restart, it reads the checkpoint file and resumes from the next date. After each date's Silver output is written idempotently (lesson 2), the checkpoint is updated. If the pipeline fails between dates (after writing Silver for January 15th but before writing the checkpoint for January 16th), the restart reprocesses January 16th — which is safe because the Silver write is idempotent. When the entire 365-day job completes, the checkpoint file is deleted. At CelticWind, the checkpoint file lives in the pipeline's data directory alongside the Silver output, and the monitoring dashboard shows the reprocessing progress (current_date / total_days).
When to Use Checkpointing

Checkpointing adds complexity (the CheckpointManager, the resume logic, the edge case where the checkpoint file itself is corrupted) and is justified only when the pipeline's runtime exceeds the cost of restarting from the beginning. At CelticWind, the threshold is 30 minutes: pipelines that take less than 30 minutes reprocess from scratch on failure (the compute cost is small). Pipelines that take more than 30 minutes use checkpointing. The daily Silver pipeline (63 seconds) does not checkpoint. The annual reprocessing job (8 hours) and the historical backfill (2 hours) both use checkpointing. The threshold is documented in the engineering guidelines and reviewed when pipeline runtimes change.

blocks.title: Checkpointing requires idempotent steps
blocks.content: The checkpoint marks a step as completed. On resume, the pipeline skips completed steps and starts from the next one. If the step that runs after the checkpoint is not idempotent (it appends instead of overwrites), a failure between the step's execution and the next checkpoint's save can produce duplicates on retry. At CelticWind, the rule is: a step must be idempotent before it can be checkpointed. The checkpoint is saved after the idempotent write completes, creating a safe resume point.

Kubernetes sends SIGTERM to a container 30 seconds before killing it. If the pipeline is mid-write when SIGTERM arrives (writing row 600,000 of 1,200,000 to a Parquet file), the kill produces a truncated file that downstream consumers cannot read. Graceful shutdown catches the SIGTERM signal, finishes the current unit of work (the current chunk or the current date's processing), saves a checkpoint, and exits cleanly. The 30-second grace period is sufficient for CelticWind's pipeline to finish the current 50,000-row chunk (which takes approximately 3 seconds) and write the checkpoint.
Signal Handling in Python

The GracefulShutdown class registers a SIGTERM handler that sets a threading.Event flag. The pipeline checks shutdown.should_stop between chunks: if the flag is set, the pipeline stops after the current chunk is fully processed and the checkpoint is saved. The remaining chunks are not processed — but the checkpoint ensures that the next run resumes from where this one stopped. At CelticWind, the grace period is 30 seconds (Kubernetes default). The longest chunk processing time is 3 seconds, so the pipeline always finishes the current chunk within the grace period.
What to Do in the Shutdown Handler

The signal handler itself should be minimal: set the flag and return. Do not perform I/O (writing files, logging extensively, making API calls) in the signal handler because signal handlers can interrupt any Python code, including code that holds locks or is mid-write. At CelticWind, the signal handler sets the event and logs one warning message. All cleanup (checkpoint save, metrics flush, connection close) happens in the main thread after the current chunk completes — not in the signal handler itself. The pipeline's finally block (from Module 7 junior's context manager pattern) handles the cleanup regardless of whether shutdown was requested or an exception occurred.

blocks.title: Test graceful shutdown explicitly
blocks.content: A shutdown handler that is never tested may not work when needed. At CelticWind, the integration test suite includes a shutdown test: start the pipeline in a subprocess, send SIGTERM after 2 seconds, wait for the process to exit, and verify that the output is not corrupted (the Parquet file is readable, the checkpoint is consistent with the written data). This test runs in the CI pipeline's integration stage and catches regressions where a code change moves the shutdown.should_stop check to the wrong location in the loop.

At 03:15, PagerDuty alerts the on-call engineer: 'celticwind_pipeline_last_success > 25 hours.' The Silver pipeline failed at 03:02 and the automatic retry also failed at 03:32. The engineer has approximately 2.5 hours before the 06:00 SLA deadline. The incident response process determines what happens in those 2.5 hours: detect the failure (already done by the alert), triage the severity (is billing data affected?), diagnose the root cause (check logs and metrics), fix the immediate problem (retry, rollback, or manual intervention), verify the fix (confirm correct output), and document the incident (post-mortem within 48 hours). Each step has a time budget; spending 2 hours on diagnosis leaves no time for the fix.
The Six-Step Incident Response

rows: Assess severity: which teams are affected, what is the SLA deadline
Grafana overview dashboard (which pipelines are red)
Identify root cause from logs, metrics, and recent changes
structlog search by run_id, Prometheus validation_duration, git log --since
Apply the correction: retry, rollback, config change, or hotfix
kubectl rollout undo, pipeline --force-reprocess, config override
Confirm the fix produced correct output
Check row counts, rejection rate, schema, compare to previous day
Write the post-mortem: timeline, root cause, remediation, prevention

blocks.content: The time budgets enforce discipline: 30 minutes for diagnosis prevents the engineer from spending 2 hours investigating while the SLA clock ticks. If diagnosis exceeds 30 minutes without identifying the root cause, the escalation policy activates: a second engineer joins, and the team considers a known-good rollback (Module 7, lesson 9) as a temporary fix while investigation continues. At CelticWind, the on-call engineer follows the runbook (lesson 6), which maps common alert types to diagnostic steps and fixes. The majority of incidents (70% at CelticWind) are resolved by the runbook without escalation.
The Triage Decision Tree

Not all incidents are equal. A failed Bronze ingestion pipeline (no new data) is high severity: the billing team has no fresh data and the SLA is at risk. A failed Gold aggregation pipeline (no updated reports) is medium severity: the billing team still has yesterday's Silver data, and the reports can be regenerated after the fix. A failed analytics enrichment pipeline (no weather correlation data) is low severity: the analytics team can work with unenriched data. At CelticWind, the severity determines the response: high severity pages the on-call engineer immediately (PagerDuty), medium severity sends a Slack alert for business-hours investigation, and low severity creates a ticket for the next sprint.

blocks.title: The post-mortem is the most valuable step
blocks.content: The fix resolves today's incident. The post-mortem prevents next month's. A well-written post-mortem answers three questions: what happened (timeline of events), why it happened (root cause analysis, not 'the API was down' but 'we had no fallback when the API was down'), and what prevents recurrence (add a cached fallback, add a circuit breaker, add a test). At CelticWind, post-mortems are blameless: they focus on systemic causes and process improvements, not on who made a mistake. The post-mortem archive is the team's institutional memory for operational failures.

An operational runbook is a step-by-step procedure for a specific operational task: 'how to reprocess a specific date,' 'how to add a new sub-region,' 'how to rotate the EPEX API key,' 'how to investigate a spike in rejections.' The runbook eliminates tribal knowledge: the on-call engineer does not need to remember the commands, the order of operations, or the verification steps — the runbook provides them. At CelticWind, every alert links to a runbook page. The on-call engineer receives the alert, opens the runbook, and follows the steps. The median time-to-resolution for runbook-covered incidents is 12 minutes; for incidents without a runbook, it is 48 minutes.
Runbook Structure

# RUNBOOK: High Rejection Rate Alert
# Trigger: celticwind_validation_rejection_rate > 1% for silver_meters pipeline

## Severity: Medium (billing data present but partially incomplete)

## Step 1: Check the rejection breakdown
- Open Grafana > Pipeline Detail > silver_meters > Rejection Breakdown panel
- Identify which field(s) account for the majority of rejections
- If one field dominates (> 80%): likely a source data change (go to Step 2)
- If rejections are spread across fields: likely a validation rule change (go to Step 3)

## Step 2: Source data change
- Check the Bronze file for the affected date: ls data/bronze/date=2025-01-15/
- Compare the file's column headers to the expected schema
- If columns changed: contact the source team (Slack #data-sources)
- If data values changed: check sample rows with: head -20 data/bronze/date=2025-01-15/*.csv

## Step 3: Validation rule change
- Check recent deployments: git log --since='2 days ago' --oneline src/celticwind/validators/
- If a recent change modified validation rules: consider rollback (see Rollback runbook)

## Verification
- After fix: rerun pipeline with --force-reprocess flag
- Verify: rejection_rate < 1% in the pipeline's validation report
- Verify: row count matches expected (within 5% of previous day)

The runbook has four sections: trigger (which alert fires and what it means), severity (how urgent is the response), diagnostic steps (a decision tree that narrows the cause from symptoms to root cause), and verification (how to confirm the fix worked). Each step includes the exact command or dashboard link — the engineer does not need to remember which Grafana panel shows the rejection breakdown or which git log command shows recent validator changes. At CelticWind, 8 runbooks cover the most common alerts: high rejection rate, zero rows processed, SLA breach, API failure, schema mismatch, memory exceeded, deployment failure, and checkpoint corruption.
Maintaining Runbooks

Runbooks decay: dashboard URLs change, commands change, and new failure modes appear that the runbook does not cover. At CelticWind, runbook maintenance follows three practices. First, every post-mortem that identifies a missing or outdated runbook step creates a ticket to update the runbook. Second, every new alert must have a runbook before it is enabled in PagerDuty — an alert without a runbook is not allowed. Third, the quarterly operations review includes a runbook walkthrough: the team reads each runbook, verifies that the commands work, and updates any stale references. The runbook is a living document, not a write-once artifact.

blocks.title: Write the runbook when you solve the problem, not after
blocks.content: The best time to write a runbook is immediately after resolving an incident, while the diagnostic steps and fix commands are fresh. At CelticWind, the post-mortem template includes a section: 'If this incident recurs, what steps should the on-call engineer follow?' The answer to that question becomes the runbook. Writing the runbook during the post-mortem takes 15 minutes. Writing it from memory a week later takes an hour and misses details. The policy is: any task performed more than twice gets a runbook.

The pipeline's validation rules change: a new constraint (wind_speed_ms must be between 0 and 60 for the GALWAY-COAST tidal meters) was added in version 1.3.0. The constraint is correct going forward, but the historical Silver data (365 days of output) was processed without it. Some historical readings have wind_speed_ms = -1 (a sensor error code) that should have been quarantined. The team needs to reprocess the historical data with the updated rules. Three reprocessing strategies provide different trade-offs between completeness, cost, and complexity.
The Three Reprocessing Strategies

rows: All Bronze data through the complete pipeline (read → validate → transform → write)
Validation rules changed, transformation logic changed, or schema changed
Only the affected date partitions (dates where the changed rule would produce different output)
Medium: minutes to hours depending on affected partition count
The change affects a known subset of dates (e.g., only dates with GALWAY-COAST data)
Only the changed step, keeping upstream and downstream intact
Low: rerun only the validation step on existing Silver data
The change is isolated to one step and does not affect the step's output schema

blocks.content: Full reprocessing is the safest: it guarantees that every day's output reflects the current pipeline logic. It is also the most expensive: CelticWind's annual reprocessing takes 8 hours (with checkpointing from lesson 3). Incremental reprocessing is targeted: reprocess only the 45 days where GALWAY-COAST tidal data exists, taking approximately 30 minutes. Targeted reprocessing is the cheapest: revalidate the existing Silver data without re-reading Bronze or re-transforming, taking approximately 10 minutes. At CelticWind, the choice depends on the change's scope: validation rule changes use incremental (reprocess affected partitions), transformation logic changes use full (the output schema may differ), and configuration changes use targeted (only the threshold value changed).
The Reprocessing Safety Checklist

Reprocessing replaces historical data that downstream consumers may have already used. Before reprocessing, the team verifies: has the Gold layer been regenerated from the affected Silver partitions (if not, Gold data is stale after reprocessing)? Has the billing team already sent invoices based on the old Silver data (if yes, reprocessing requires a billing correction process)? Is the reprocessing pipeline version the same as the production version (if not, reprocessing may introduce unrelated changes)? At CelticWind, the reprocessing checklist is a prerequisite in the reprocessing runbook: the engineer must confirm all three checks before starting the reprocess. The checklist prevents the common mistake of reprocessing Silver data and forgetting to regenerate Gold, leaving the reporting layer inconsistent with the data layer.

blocks.title: Reprocessing without idempotency is data corruption
blocks.content: If the Silver write is not idempotent (lesson 2), reprocessing a date appends new data alongside the existing output instead of replacing it. If the reprocessing job fails midway, some dates have the old output, some have the new output, and some have both. At CelticWind, the reprocessing runbook's first step is: 'Verify that the pipeline uses idempotent writes (atomic overwrite per partition). If not, do not reprocess — escalate to the platform team.'

An SLA (Service Level Agreement) is a commitment from the data engineering team to its consumers: the Silver data will be available by 06:00 UTC, at least 99.9% of expected records will be present, and the rejection rate will be below 1%. Without a formal SLA, the billing team's expectation is 'the data should be there when I need it' — a vague standard that is impossible to meet consistently. With a formal SLA, both sides agree on specific, measurable targets. The engineering team knows what to optimize for (freshness, completeness, quality), and the billing team knows what to expect (and when to escalate if expectations are not met).
CelticWind's SLA Dimensions

rows: Not completed by 05:00 (1 hour buffer for investigation)

blocks.content: Each dimension has a target (the commitment), a measurement (the metric that tracks it), and an alert threshold (the point at which the engineering team investigates before the SLA is breached). The alert thresholds are set inside the SLA boundaries: the freshness alert fires at 05:00, providing 1 hour of investigation time before the 06:00 SLA deadline. The completeness alert fires at 99.5%, providing a 0.4% buffer before the 99.9% SLA boundary. At CelticWind, the SLA dashboard (part of the Grafana overview from Module 9) shows a 30-day rolling view of each dimension, with green/yellow/red status based on the proximity to the SLA boundary.
SLA Reporting to Consumers

The SLA is only meaningful if consumers can verify it. At CelticWind, the weekly data quality report (emailed to the billing, analytics, and operations teams) includes the SLA compliance for each dimension over the past 7 days: 7/7 runs completed by 06:00, completeness averaged 99.97%, rejection rate averaged 0.04%, and availability was 100%. If any dimension breached its target, the report includes the incident summary and the remediation taken. This transparency builds trust: the consumers see that the engineering team measures, monitors, and responds to quality issues — not just claims that 'the data is reliable' without evidence.

blocks.title: SLAs should be negotiated, not dictated
blocks.content: A 06:00 freshness SLA is meaningless if the billing team actually needs the data by 04:00 for their own processing deadline. An SLA that the engineering team defines unilaterally may not match the consumer's actual needs. At CelticWind, SLAs are negotiated in quarterly planning meetings between the engineering team and each consuming team. The billing team's platinum SLA (06:00 freshness, 99.9% completeness) reflects their invoicing deadline. The analytics team's gold SLA (09:00 freshness, 99% completeness) reflects their less urgent exploratory workload.

CelticWind's 1.2 million meters are growing at 15% annually as new wind farms and tidal installations come online. At this rate, the meter count reaches 1.38 million next year, 1.59 million in year two, and 2.38 million in year five. The Silver pipeline currently takes 63 seconds with a 300-second SLA — 79% headroom. But runtime does not grow linearly with data: some operations scale as O(n) (reading, writing), some scale as O(n log n) (sorting), and some may have hidden O(n²) components that only surface at larger scale. Capacity planning projects when the pipeline will breach its SLA under realistic growth assumptions and identifies the infrastructure changes needed before that point.
Projecting Pipeline Runtime Growth

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class CapacityProjection:
    """Project pipeline runtime at future data volumes."""
    current_rows: int
    current_runtime_s: float
    sla_seconds: float
    annual_growth_rate: float

    def project(self, years: int) -> list[dict]:
        projections = []
        rows = self.current_rows
        for year in range(1, years + 1):
            rows = int(rows * (1 + self.annual_growth_rate))
            # Assume linear scaling (conservative; may be worse)
            runtime = self.current_runtime_s * (rows / self.current_rows)
            headroom = 1 - (runtime / self.sla_seconds)
            projections.append({
                'year': year, 'rows': rows,
                'projected_runtime_s': round(runtime, 1),
                'sla_headroom_pct': round(headroom * 100, 1),
                'action_needed': headroom < 0.2,  # < 20% headroom
            })
        return projections

proj = CapacityProjection(
    current_rows=1_200_000, current_runtime_s=63,
    sla_seconds=300, annual_growth_rate=0.15,
)
for p in proj.project(5):
    print(f"Year {p['year']}: {p['rows']:,} rows, {p['projected_runtime_s']}s, "
          f"headroom: {p['sla_headroom_pct']}%, action: {p['action_needed']}")
# Year 1: 1,380,000 rows, 72.5s, headroom: 75.8%, action: False
# Year 2: 1,587,000 rows, 83.3s, headroom: 72.2%, action: False
# Year 3: 1,825,050 rows, 95.8s, headroom: 68.1%, action: False
# Year 4: 2,098,808 rows, 110.2s, headroom: 63.3%, action: False
# Year 5: 2,413,629 rows, 126.7s, headroom: 57.8%, action: False

The projection assumes linear runtime scaling — the conservative case. If any pipeline step has superlinear scaling (the O(n²) deduplication from Module 8 before optimization), the actual growth will be faster than projected. At CelticWind, the capacity projection is run quarterly with the actual growth rate (measured from the last 12 months of rows_processed metrics) and the actual runtime (measured from the pipeline_duration_seconds metric). The projection identifies the year when headroom drops below 20% — the threshold where proactive optimization should begin. For the current pipeline, the 20% threshold is approximately year 8, providing comfortable planning horizon.
Planning Infrastructure Changes

When the capacity projection shows headroom approaching 20%, the team evaluates three categories of response. First, optimize the pipeline: the Module 8 diagnostic workflow identifies the current bottleneck and the potential improvement (can we gain another 2x from algorithmic changes or Polars optimizations?). Second, scale the infrastructure: can the server be upgraded from 4 GB to 8 GB RAM, or from 4 cores to 8 cores (enabling more parallel workers in Module 2)? Third, change the architecture: should the pipeline move from single-server Python to distributed PySpark, which handles data growth by adding cluster nodes rather than optimizing single-node code? At CelticWind, the progression is: optimize first (cheapest), scale second (moderate cost), re-architect last (expensive but necessary at 10x growth).

blocks.title: Capacity planning uses metrics from every previous module
blocks.content: The rows_processed counter (Module 9) provides the data growth rate. The pipeline_duration_seconds gauge (Module 9) provides the runtime trend. The performance baseline (Module 8) identifies which steps scale linearly and which may scale superlinearly. The profiling workflow (Module 8) identifies the bottleneck to optimize. The concurrent pipeline (Module 2) determines how much the infrastructure can be parallelized. The memory budget (Module 3) determines the maximum data volume per chunk. Capacity planning is the synthesis of operational data from every module in the track.

This is the final lesson of the Python for Data Engineers mid-level track. Over 10 modules and 100 lessons, you have transformed CelticWind's pipeline from a junior-level script that worked on one developer's laptop into a production-grade system that 3 teams operate across 12 pipelines with SLA commitments, automated deployment, and 24/7 monitoring. Each module added a layer to the production capability: the architecture that makes pipelines extensible (Module 1), the concurrency that makes them fast (Module 2), the memory efficiency that makes them scalable (Module 3), the framework that eliminates duplication (Module 4), the configuration that makes them portable (Module 5), the testing that proves they work (Module 6), the CI/CD that deploys them safely (Module 7), the profiling that keeps them fast (Module 8), the observability that makes them visible (Module 9), and the operational discipline that keeps them reliable over years (this module).
The Production Playbook

rows: 12 pipelines sharing one framework via protocol-based interfaces
Threading for I/O, multiprocessing for CPU
12 production incidents prevented by quality gates
Tuesday incident: detected in 15 seconds (was 4 hours)

blocks.content: The playbook is not a checklist to implement all at once — it is a reference for which concern to address when the need arises. A team with 3 pipelines needs Modules 1, 6, and 7 (architecture, testing, deployment) before Modules 4 and 5 (framework, configuration). A team hitting memory limits needs Module 3 before Module 8 (memory efficiency before CPU profiling). A team with frequent production incidents needs Modules 9 and 10 (observability, operations) before Module 2 (concurrency). The playbook's value is knowing which module provides which capability — and reaching for the right module when the need emerges.

The CelticWind scenario has grown from 400,000 meters and 1 team (junior track) to 1.2 million meters, 8 sub-regions, 3 teams, and 12 pipelines (mid-level track). The senior track continues the story: CelticWind merges with Scottish and Breton energy companies to form the Atlantic Energy Alliance, scaling to 5 million meters across 3 countries with 25 sub-regions, 10 teams, and 200+ pipelines. The patterns from this track — protocols, frameworks, observability, operational discipline — are the foundation that the senior track builds on. The architecture designed here becomes the platform architecture; the configuration system becomes the multi-tenant configuration; the observability stack becomes the enterprise monitoring infrastructure.

blocks.title: You can now operate what you build
blocks.content: The junior track taught you to build pipelines. This track taught you to operate them: to make them fast, testable, deployable, observable, and reliable. The shift is from 'I can write code that processes data' to 'I can build systems that 3 teams rely on for billing, analytics, and operations — with SLA commitments, automated deployment, and incident response procedures.' The next track (senior) shifts again: from operating systems to designing platforms that other teams build on. But the operational discipline from this track — idempotency, monitoring, runbooks, capacity planning — remains the foundation for every level of engineering maturity.

modules.title: Advanced OOP for Pipeline Architecture
modules.description: Design pipeline architectures using protocols, abstract base classes, composition, and dependency injection — the patterns that make pipeline code extensible, testable, and team-scalable.

sections.title: Why Junior-Level Architecture Breaks at Scale

blocks.content: CelticWind's junior-level pipeline architecture followed the four-module pattern from the junior track: readers.py, validators.py, transformers.py, and writers.py in a single celticwind package. This worked for 1 team, 4 regions, and 3 pipelines. CelticWind now has 3 teams (billing, analytics, operations), 8 sub-regions, and 12 pipelines. The four-module pattern has not scaled: each team copied the original modules and modified them for their data sources, producing 3 diverging copies of the validation logic, 3 diverging copies of the reader logic, and 3 diverging sets of tests. When Aoife fixed a timezone bug in the validation module, she had to apply the same fix to all 3 copies — and the analytics team's copy had already diverged enough that the patch did not apply cleanly, introducing a new bug that took 2 days to diagnose.
The Three Symptoms of Architectural Breakdown

rows: Functions are concrete implementations that cannot be shared without copying the entire module
Define interfaces (protocols) that all teams implement, with shared logic in a common base
The pipeline function calls readers and API clients directly, requiring real files and network access to test
Inject dependencies as parameters so tests can substitute mock implementations
The pipeline is a linear script: read → validate → transform → write, hardcoded in sequence
Define steps as composable objects that the pipeline assembles from configuration

blocks.content: Each symptom points to the same root cause: the junior-level architecture couples implementation to interface. The pipeline function knows that it reads CSV files (because it calls read_csv_meters directly), knows that it validates with Pydantic (because it instantiates MeterReadingModel directly), and knows that it writes Parquet (because it calls write_parquet directly). This coupling means changing any of these decisions — reading from an API instead of CSV, validating with a different model, writing to a database instead of Parquet — requires modifying the pipeline function itself. At 3 pipelines, this coupling is manageable. At 12 pipelines across 3 teams, it produces the duplication, untestability, and rigidity that CelticWind is experiencing.
What This Module Teaches

This module teaches the OOP patterns that decouple implementation from interface: protocols define what a component does without specifying how (lesson 2), dependency injection passes components as parameters instead of hardcoding them (lesson 5), composition builds complex components from simple ones (lesson 4), and the strategy pattern makes behaviors swappable at runtime (lesson 6). By the end of the module, CelticWind's pipeline will accept any reader, any validator, any transformer, and any writer — without modification. The 12 pipelines will share the pipeline infrastructure and differ only in their configured components.

blocks.title: The shift from writing pipelines to designing pipeline architectures
blocks.content: The junior-level track taught you to build a pipeline. This module teaches you to build the architecture that pipelines plug into. The difference is the audience: you are no longer writing code for yourself — you are writing code that 3 teams and 15 engineers will extend, test, and maintain. Every pattern in this module exists because someone else will use your code, and they should not need to understand (or modify) your implementation to do so.

A protocol defines what a component can do without requiring the component to inherit from a specific class. The DataReader protocol declares: any object with a read(path: Path) -> list[dict] method is a valid reader — whether it is a CsvReader, a ParquetReader, a JsonReader, or a MockReader for testing. The pipeline depends on the DataReader protocol, not on any specific implementation. This means you can swap implementations without changing the pipeline, add new implementations without modifying existing code, and test with mock implementations that never touch the file system.
Defining a Protocol

Each protocol declares a single method with its signature: parameter types and return type. The @runtime_checkable decorator enables isinstance() checks at runtime (isinstance(csv_reader, DataReader) returns True if csv_reader has a read method with the right signature). The ... in the method body means 'no implementation here — the protocol only declares the interface.' Any class that has the declared method with the right signature automatically satisfies the protocol — no inheritance, no registration, no explicit declaration. This is structural typing: the structure of the object (its methods) determines its type, not its class hierarchy.
Implementing a Protocol

CsvReader and ParquetReader have no common base class. They do not inherit from DataReader. They do not even import DataReader. Yet both satisfy the protocol because both have a read(path: Path) -> list[dict] method. This is the power of structural typing: the implementations are completely independent of each other and of the protocol definition. A new reader (ApiReader, DatabaseReader, MockReader) can be written by any team at any time, in any package, and it automatically satisfies the protocol if it has the right method signature. At CelticWind, the three teams maintain their own readers (CsvReader for billing, ParquetReader for analytics, ApiReader for operations) and all plug into the same pipeline infrastructure.

blocks.title: Protocols are Python's answer to Go interfaces
blocks.content: If you have used Go, protocols are familiar: they define interfaces through method signatures, and any type that has those methods satisfies the interface implicitly. If you have used Java, protocols differ from interfaces: Java interfaces require explicit implements declarations, while Python protocols are satisfied structurally. The practical benefit for data engineering: you can define a protocol in your pipeline framework, and third-party readers (from an open-source library or another team's package) satisfy it without modification — as long as they have the right methods.

Protocols define interfaces structurally: any class with the right methods satisfies the protocol implicitly. Abstract Base Classes (ABCs) define interfaces nominally: a class must explicitly inherit from the ABC and implement its abstract methods. Both achieve the same goal — separating interface from implementation — but they differ in enforcement timing, error messages, and the ability to share implementation code. Understanding when to use each is essential because CelticWind's pipeline architecture uses both: protocols for external interfaces (where third-party code must conform) and ABCs for internal framework components (where the team controls all implementations and benefits from shared base behavior).
An Abstract Base Class

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from pathlib import Path
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class BaseTransformer(ABC):
    """Abstract base for all CelticWind transformers.

    Provides shared logging and timing. Subclasses implement transform_records().
    """

    def transform(self, records: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
        """Transform records with automatic logging and timing."""
        logger.info('%s: transforming %d records', self.__class__.__name__, len(records))
        import time
        start = time.time()
        result = self.transform_records(records)  # delegate to subclass
        duration = time.time() - start
        logger.info('%s: produced %d records in %.2fs', self.__class__.__name__, len(result), duration)
        return result

    @abstractmethod
    def transform_records(self, records: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
        """Subclasses implement this method with their transformation logic."""
        ...


class RegionNormalizer(BaseTransformer):
    """Normalizes region codes to uppercase."""

    def transform_records(self, records: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
        return [{**r, 'region': r['region'].upper()} for r in records]


class DerivedFieldAdder(BaseTransformer):
    """Adds computed fields (daily_mwh) to records."""

    def transform_records(self, records: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
        return [{**r, 'daily_mwh': float(r['daily_kw']) / 1000} for r in records]

BaseTransformer is an ABC with one concrete method (transform, which handles logging and timing) and one abstract method (transform_records, which subclasses implement). The concrete method provides shared behavior that every transformer gets for free — without each subclass reimplementing the logging and timing boilerplate. This is the key advantage of ABCs over protocols: ABCs can share implementation code through the base class. RegionNormalizer and DerivedFieldAdder implement only their specific transformation logic; the logging and timing come from the base class. Attempting to instantiate BaseTransformer() directly raises TypeError: Can't instantiate abstract class — the abstract method enforcement happens at creation time, not at usage time.
The Decision Framework

left.label: Use Protocols when
points: External code must conform (third-party readers, plugins from other teams)
You want structural typing (any class with the right methods works)
No shared implementation is needed (each implementation is independent)
Error detection at usage time is acceptable (wrong type caught when the method is called)

right.label: Use ABCs when
points: Internal code benefits from shared behavior (transformers with common logging)
You want nominal typing (subclasses must explicitly inherit)
Shared implementation reduces duplication (logging, timing, error handling in the base class)
Error detection at creation time is preferred (missing method caught at instantiation)

blocks.content: At CelticWind, the architecture uses both patterns in a layered approach. The top-level pipeline interface uses protocols: DataReader, DataWriter, DataValidator are protocols that any team's code can satisfy without importing the framework. The internal framework components use ABCs: BaseTransformer, BaseValidator provide shared logging, timing, and error handling that all internal implementations inherit. This layered approach gives external interfaces maximum flexibility through structural typing and gives internal components maximum code reuse through shared base class behavior — combining the strengths of both patterns.

blocks.title: Do not use ABCs as a substitute for protocols when no shared code exists
blocks.content: An ABC with only abstract methods (no concrete shared behavior) is a protocol with extra ceremony: it requires explicit inheritance and provides nothing in return. If the base class has no concrete methods, use a Protocol instead. At CelticWind, this rule is enforced in code review: every ABC must have at least one concrete method that provides shared behavior. ABCs with only abstract methods are refactored to protocols.

The junior-level track used single-purpose functions: read_csv_meters reads CSV, validate_readings validates, normalize_timestamps normalizes. At mid-level scale, these functions acquire options: the reader needs to handle different encodings, different delimiters, optional schema validation, and optional deduplication. The naive approach adds parameters to the function until it has 15 parameters covering every combination. The inheritance approach creates a class hierarchy: BaseReader → FileReader → CsvReader → ValidatingCsvReader → DeduplicatingValidatingCsvReader — a 5-level hierarchy where each level adds one capability. Both approaches become unmanageable. Composition provides a better alternative: small, focused components that are combined at construction time.
The Deep-Inheritance Problem

# BAD: deep inheritance — fragile, rigid, hard to test
class BaseReader: ...
class FileReader(BaseReader): ...          # adds file opening
class CsvReader(FileReader): ...           # adds CSV parsing
class ValidatingCsvReader(CsvReader): ...  # adds schema validation
class DeduplicatingValidatingCsvReader(ValidatingCsvReader): ...  # adds dedup

# Problem 1: What if you need deduplication WITHOUT validation?
#   → You cannot skip a level in the hierarchy.
# Problem 2: What if you need validation on Parquet (not CSV)?
#   → ValidatingCsvReader is hardcoded to CSV.
# Problem 3: What if testing needs a reader that validates but does not read files?
#   → Every level depends on the levels above it.
The Composition Alternative

from pathlib import Path

class CsvReader:
    """Reads CSV files. Does ONLY reading."""
    def __init__(self, encoding: str = 'utf-8', delimiter: str = ','):
        self.encoding = encoding
        self.delimiter = delimiter

    def read(self, path: Path) -> list[dict]:
        import csv
        with open(path, 'r', encoding=self.encoding, newline='') as f:
            return list(csv.DictReader(f, delimiter=self.delimiter))


class ValidatingReader:
    """Wraps any reader and adds validation after reading."""
    def __init__(self, reader: DataReader, validator: DataValidator):
        self.reader = reader
        self.validator = validator

    def read(self, path: Path) -> list[dict]:
        raw = self.reader.read(path)          # delegate reading
        valid, rejected = self.validator.validate(raw)  # validate
        return valid


# Compose at construction time — mix and match freely
csv_reader = CsvReader(encoding='utf-8')
validating_csv = ValidatingReader(csv_reader, PydanticValidator())

# Parquet with validation — same validator, different reader
parquet_reader = ParquetReader(columns=['meter_id', 'daily_kw'])
validating_parquet = ValidatingReader(parquet_reader, PydanticValidator())

Composition wraps components inside other components: ValidatingReader wraps any DataReader and any DataValidator, composing their capabilities at construction time. You can have validation with CSV, validation with Parquet, validation with API, or no validation at all — every combination works because the components are independent. Adding deduplication requires a DeduplicatingReader that wraps any DataReader, not a new level in an inheritance hierarchy. At CelticWind, the 12 pipelines compose their components differently: the billing pipeline uses ValidatingReader(CsvReader(), StrictValidator()), the analytics pipeline uses ParquetReader() without validation (the SCADA system validates upstream), and the operations pipeline uses ValidatingReader(ApiReader(), LenientValidator()).

The key insight: composition achieves code reuse through delegation (one object delegates to another), not through inheritance (one class extends another). Delegation is flexible because the delegate can be any object that satisfies the protocol — including a test mock. Inheritance is rigid because the parent class is fixed at class definition time. At CelticWind, the rule is: inherit for shared behavior (ABCs from lesson 3), compose for capability combination (wrapping readers with validators, transformers with loggers, writers with retriers).

blocks.title: Composition enables the 'decorator' pattern for pipelines
blocks.content: ValidatingReader is a decorator — it wraps a reader and adds behavior (validation) without modifying the original reader. You can stack decorators: LoggingReader(ValidatingReader(RetryingReader(CsvReader()))) adds logging, validation, and retry around the base CSV reader. Each decorator is a single-responsibility wrapper, and the composition order determines the behavior order. This is the same pattern that Python's @decorator syntax uses — applied to pipeline components instead of functions.

The junior-level pipeline function hardcodes its dependencies: it calls read_csv_meters() directly, imports MeterReadingModel explicitly, and creates the Parquet writer inline. Testing this function requires a real CSV file on disk, a real Pydantic model, and a writable Parquet path — making the 'unit test' into an integration test that depends on the file system, the schema definition, and the output directory. Dependency injection (DI) solves this by passing dependencies as constructor or function parameters instead of hardcoding them. The pipeline receives its reader, validator, and writer from the outside — and the test provides mock implementations that require no file system, no schema, and no output directory.
Before and After DI

The SilverPipeline class receives its reader, validator, and writer as constructor parameters. In production, the caller passes CsvReader(), PydanticValidator(), and ParquetWriter(). In tests, the caller passes InMemoryReader(test_data), AlwaysValidValidator(), and NullWriter() — mock implementations that run instantly without touching the file system. The pipeline function's logic (read → validate → write) is identical in both cases; only the component implementations differ. This separation means the pipeline logic can be tested in complete isolation, without the overhead, fragility, and slowness of file system I/O.
Testing with DI

The test creates an InMemoryReader that returns predetermined test data (no CSV file needed), a real PydanticValidator (testing the actual validation logic), and a CollectingWriter that captures the output in memory (no Parquet file needed). The test verifies the pipeline's orchestration logic: 2 records in, 1 valid out, 1 rejected. The test runs in milliseconds because no file system I/O occurs. At CelticWind, every pipeline class uses constructor injection, and every pipeline test uses in-memory readers and collecting writers. The test suite runs in 3 seconds; with file-based tests, it would take 45 seconds.

blocks.title: DI does not mean a DI framework
blocks.content: Python's DI pattern is manual constructor injection: pass dependencies as __init__ parameters. You do not need a DI framework (like Java's Spring or .NET's Autofac). The framework's job — wiring the right implementations to the right constructors — is handled by a factory function or the pipeline entry point. At CelticWind, a create_pipeline(config) factory function reads the configuration, instantiates the configured reader/validator/writer, and passes them to SilverPipeline(). The factory is the only place where concrete classes are instantiated; everywhere else, the code works with protocols.

sections.title: The Strategy Pattern for Pipeline Steps

blocks.content: The billing team validates meter readings strictly: any reading with daily_kw outside [0, 5000] is rejected. The analytics team validates leniently: readings with daily_kw outside the range are flagged with a warning but not rejected, because the analytics team wants to study outlier patterns. The junior-level approach: two separate validation functions (validate_strict and validate_lenient) and two separate pipeline scripts that call the appropriate one. The Strategy pattern eliminates this duplication: the pipeline receives a validation strategy as a parameter, and the strategy determines the validation behavior. The pipeline code is identical; only the strategy differs.
Defining Validation Strategies

Both strategies satisfy the ValidationStrategy protocol: they have a validate method that takes a record and returns a (bool, str | None) tuple. The StrictValidator rejects out-of-range readings; the LenientValidator warns but accepts. The pipeline does not know which strategy it is using — it calls strategy.validate(record) and acts on the result. At CelticWind, the validation strategy is configured in the pipeline's YAML file: strategy: strict for billing, strategy: lenient for analytics. The factory function reads the strategy name and instantiates the appropriate class.
Using Strategies in the Pipeline

BatchValidator is a pipeline component that applies any ValidationStrategy to a batch of records. The strategy is injected at construction time through the constructor parameter. The billing pipeline creates BatchValidator(StrictValidator()), and the analytics pipeline creates BatchValidator(LenientValidator()). Both pipelines use the same BatchValidator class, the same iteration loop, and the same valid/rejected return structure. The only difference in behavior comes from the injected strategy — one algorithm structure with interchangeable validation logic. This is the essence of the Strategy pattern: varying behavior through composition rather than conditional branching.

blocks.title: Strategies are configurable behavior, not configurable values
blocks.content: The junior-level approach to configurable validation was a parameter: validate_readings(readings, max_kw=5000). This configures a value (the threshold) but not the behavior (reject vs warn). The Strategy pattern configures the behavior itself: StrictValidator rejects, LenientValidator warns. When the analytics team needs a new behavior (reject negative values but accept high values), they write a new strategy class — no modification to BatchValidator, no modification to the pipeline, no risk of breaking the billing team's validation.

sections.title: The Repository Pattern for Data Access

blocks.content: The junior-level pipeline writes Parquet files to a local directory: write_parquet(data, Path('/data/silver/meters/')). In development, the path is ./data/silver/. In staging, it is /mnt/staging/silver/. In production, it might be an Azure Blob Storage container (abfss://silver@celticwind.dfs.core.windows.net/meters/). The pipeline code must change for each environment because the storage mechanism is hardcoded into the write function. The Repository pattern abstracts data access behind a uniform interface: the pipeline calls repository.save(data, 'meters/2025-01-15') and the repository implementation handles the storage details — local files in development, Azure Blob in production.
The Repository Protocol

The DataRepository protocol declares three methods: save, load, and exists. LocalParquetRepository implements these for local Parquet files. An AzureBlobRepository (not shown) would implement the same methods using the Azure SDK. An InMemoryRepository (for testing) stores data in a dictionary. The pipeline receives a DataRepository and calls save/load/exists without knowing whether the data goes to a local file, a cloud blob, or an in-memory dictionary. At CelticWind, the factory function creates LocalParquetRepository for development and AzureBlobRepository for production — the pipeline code is identical in both environments.
Repository in the Pipeline

The pipeline uses two repositories: silver_repo for valid output and quarantine_repo for rejected records. Both could be the same type (LocalParquetRepository with different base directories) or different types (LocalParquetRepository for Silver, LocalJsonRepository for quarantine). In tests, both are InMemoryRepository instances. The pipeline does not know or care about the storage implementation — it calls save and load, and the repository handles the rest. This abstraction eliminates the environment-specific path logic from the pipeline and moves it to the factory function where it belongs.

blocks.title: Do not over-abstract storage
blocks.content: The Repository pattern adds a layer of indirection. If the pipeline only ever writes local Parquet files (no cloud storage, no alternative formats), the repository adds complexity without benefit — the junior-level write_parquet(data, path) is simpler. At CelticWind, the Repository pattern was adopted when the team added Azure Blob Storage for production — not preemptively. The rule: abstract when you have two or more concrete implementations, not when you have one implementation and a theoretical future need.

The junior-level track used a single MeterReading dataclass for the Silver layer. At mid-level scale, CelticWind needs a family of related data models: a base reading (the EEDA standard fields), a validated reading (with validation metadata), an enriched reading (with weather and price data), and a Gold summary (aggregated by region and date). These models share fields but have different purposes. Dataclass inheritance enables sharing the common fields; dataclass composition enables embedding related models as fields within a parent model.
Dataclass Inheritance

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class BaseMeterReading:
    """EEDA standard fields — shared by all reading models."""
    meter_id: str
    daily_kw: float
    date: str
    region: str
    customer_type: str = 'UNKNOWN'

@dataclass
class ValidatedReading(BaseMeterReading):
    """A reading that has passed validation."""
    daily_mwh: float = 0.0
    validated_at: str = ''
    validation_version: str = '1.0'

@dataclass
class EnrichedReading(ValidatedReading):
    """A validated reading enriched with external data."""
    price_eur_mwh: float | None = None
    temp_c: float | None = None
    wind_speed: float | None = None

# Usage: progressive enrichment through the pipeline
base = BaseMeterReading('IE-DUB-00142', 28.5, '2025-01-15', 'DUBLIN')
validated = ValidatedReading('IE-DUB-00142', 28.5, '2025-01-15', 'DUBLIN', daily_mwh=0.0285)
enriched = EnrichedReading('IE-DUB-00142', 28.5, '2025-01-15', 'DUBLIN', daily_mwh=0.0285, price_eur_mwh=45.20)

The inheritance chain mirrors the pipeline's data flow: BaseMeterReading at Bronze (the raw EEDA standard fields), ValidatedReading at Silver (with validation metadata and derived fields), and EnrichedReading at Gold (with external data from weather and price APIs). Each level adds fields without duplicating the base fields. A function that accepts BaseMeterReading also accepts ValidatedReading and EnrichedReading — the Liskov Substitution Principle in practice. At CelticWind, the base model lives in a shared package that all 3 teams import; the validated and enriched models live in the pipeline-specific packages.
Dataclass Composition

PipelineRunResult composes RegionalSummary as a list field — the result contains the summaries, it does not inherit from them. Composition is the right choice here because a pipeline run result is not a kind of summary; it contains summaries alongside other metadata. The frozen=True on RegionalSummary ensures the Gold summaries are immutable once created — they represent final, auditable output that should never be modified. The @property decorators compute derived values (duration, rejection rate) without storing them, keeping the dataclass lightweight.

blocks.title: Inherit for 'is-a', compose for 'has-a'
blocks.content: An EnrichedReading IS a ValidatedReading (with extra fields) — inheritance. A PipelineRunResult HAS RegionalSummaries (among other data) — composition. This distinction guides every data model design decision. At CelticWind, the team tests the relationship by asking: 'Can I pass this child object anywhere the parent is expected?' If yes, inheritance. If no, composition.

The pipeline's reader protocol returns list[dict], and the validator protocol accepts list[dict]. This works but loses type precision: the dict could contain any keys with any types. When the pipeline handles both meter readings and turbine telemetry, the type system cannot distinguish between them — both are list[dict]. Generic types solve this by parameterizing the pipeline components over the data type: a PipelineStep[BaseMeterReading, ValidatedReading] transforms meter readings, and a PipelineStep[TurbineTelemetry, ValidatedTelemetry] transforms turbine data. The type checker catches errors where meter reading steps are accidentally connected to turbine telemetry steps.
Generic Pipeline Steps

from typing import Generic, TypeVar, Protocol

TInput = TypeVar('TInput')
TOutput = TypeVar('TOutput')

class PipelineStep(Protocol[TInput, TOutput]):
    """A typed pipeline step: TInput in, TOutput out."""
    def process(self, data: list[TInput]) -> list[TOutput]:
        ...

class MeterValidator:
    """Validates meter readings: BaseMeterReading → ValidatedReading."""
    def process(self, data: list[BaseMeterReading]) -> list[ValidatedReading]:
        return [
            ValidatedReading(
                meter_id=r.meter_id, daily_kw=r.daily_kw, date=r.date,
                region=r.region, daily_mwh=r.daily_kw / 1000,
                validated_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
            )
            for r in data
            if r.daily_kw >= 0
        ]

# The type checker knows:
# - MeterValidator.process takes list[BaseMeterReading]
# - MeterValidator.process returns list[ValidatedReading]
# Connecting it to a turbine step would be a type error

The PipelineStep[TInput, TOutput] protocol is generic over two type parameters: the input type and the output type. MeterValidator satisfies PipelineStep[BaseMeterReading, ValidatedReading] because its process method takes list[BaseMeterReading] and returns list[ValidatedReading]. If a developer accidentally connects a MeterValidator output to a step that expects list[TurbineTelemetry], the type checker (mypy) catches the mismatch before the code runs. At CelticWind, the generic pipeline step is the foundation of the pipeline framework (Module 4): each step declares its input and output types, and the framework verifies that connected steps have compatible types.
Type Narrowing with isinstance

Type narrowing is the type checker's ability to infer a more specific type after a runtime check. After isinstance(record, BaseMeterReading), the type checker knows that record is a BaseMeterReading (not TurbineTelemetry), so accessing record.meter_id and record.daily_kw is safe. Without the isinstance check, the type checker would report an error because meter_id is not defined on the union type. At CelticWind, type narrowing is used in the pipeline framework's dispatch logic: the framework routes records to the appropriate step based on their runtime type.

blocks.title: Use generics for framework code, concrete types for business code
blocks.content: Generic types are powerful for framework components (PipelineStep[T, U], DataRepository[T]) where the types are determined by the user of the framework. Business logic code (the meter validation function, the regional aggregation function) should use concrete types (BaseMeterReading, RegionalSummary). Over-genericizing business code makes it harder to read without adding value — the meter validator will never process turbine telemetry, so making it generic over 'any record type' adds abstraction without benefit.

This module taught the OOP patterns that transform junior-level pipeline code into a mid-level pipeline architecture: protocols for interface definitions that enable structural typing, ABCs for shared base behavior with enforcement at creation time, composition for combining capabilities without deep inheritance hierarchies, dependency injection for testability through constructor parameters, the strategy pattern for configurable behavior (strict vs lenient validation), the repository pattern for environment-agnostic storage, dataclass inheritance and composition for typed data models, and generics for type-safe pipeline step connections. This lesson assembles all the patterns into CelticWind's production architecture.
The Architecture Diagram

blocks.title: CelticWind mid-level pipeline architecture
blocks.content:
Pipeline Factory (creates from config)
                              │
                    ┌─────────┴─────────┐
                    │   SilverPipeline    │  (receives all deps via DI)
                    │                     │
                    │  reader: DataReader  │───▶ CsvReader / ParquetReader / ApiReader
                    │  validator: DataVal  │───▶ BatchValidator(StrictValidator)
                    │  transformer: Base   │───▶ RegionNormalizer → DerivedFieldAdder
                    │  silver_repo: Repo   │───▶ LocalParquetRepo / AzureBlobRepo
                    │  quarantine: Repo    │───▶ LocalJsonRepo
                    └─────────────────────┘
                              │
              All implementations are swappable:
              • Production: CsvReader + StrictValidator + AzureBlobRepo
              • Analytics:  ParquetReader + LenientValidator + AzureBlobRepo
              • Tests:      InMemoryReader + PydanticValidator + InMemoryRepo
blocks.caption: The SilverPipeline class receives all dependencies through its constructor. The Pipeline Factory reads the YAML configuration and instantiates the appropriate implementations. In production, the factory creates CsvReader + StrictValidator + AzureBlobRepo. In tests, it creates InMemoryReader + PydanticValidator + InMemoryRepo. The pipeline logic is identical in both cases.

blocks.content: The architecture has three layers. The protocol layer defines the interfaces: DataReader, DataValidator, DataWriter, DataRepository. The implementation layer provides the concrete classes: CsvReader, ParquetReader, StrictValidator, LenientValidator, LocalParquetRepository, AzureBlobRepository. The composition layer assembles the components: SilverPipeline receives its dependencies from the factory, and the factory reads the YAML configuration to determine which implementations to use. Adding a new reader (DatabaseReader for a new data source) requires writing the class and registering it in the factory — no modification to the pipeline, the protocols, or any existing implementations.

The architecture solves the three symptoms from lesson 1. Duplicated logic: all 12 pipelines use the same SilverPipeline class with different configurations, eliminating the 3 diverging copies of validation code. Untestable code: every pipeline test uses in-memory readers and collecting writers, and the test suite runs in 3 seconds instead of 45 seconds. Rigid step ordering: the pipeline's steps are injected as dependencies, not hardcoded — swapping the validation strategy or the storage backend requires changing the YAML configuration, not the pipeline code.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 2 addresses the next production bottleneck: the sequential pipeline that reads 8 regional CSV files one at a time.
