The architecture from this module makes concurrency natural: the DataReader protocol does not specify whether reading is sequential or concurrent — a ConcurrentCsvReader that reads files in parallel satisfies the same protocol. The pipeline receives the concurrent reader through dependency injection and processes 8 files in parallel without any change to the pipeline's orchestration code. Architecture enables concurrency; concurrency does not require architectural changes.

modules.title: Concurrency and Parallelism
modules.description: Process data concurrently using threading for I/O-bound tasks, multiprocessing for CPU-bound tasks, and asyncio for high-concurrency API integration — turning sequential bottlenecks into parallel speedups.

blocks.content: CelticWind's Silver pipeline reads 8 daily CSV files — one per sub-region. Each file averages 150,000 rows and takes 12 seconds to read, validate, and transform. The sequential pipeline processes them one at a time: read DUBLIN-NORTH (12s), read DUBLIN-SOUTH (12s), read CORK-EAST (12s), and so on for all 8 files. Total I/O time: 96 seconds. But during each 12-second read, the CPU is mostly idle — waiting for the disk to deliver bytes. The 8 reads are independent: CORK-EAST does not depend on DUBLIN-NORTH's result. If the pipeline reads all 8 files simultaneously, the I/O overlaps and the total time drops to approximately 15 seconds — an 84% reduction from a change in execution strategy, not a change in logic.
Mapping the Time Budget

import time
from pathlib import Path

def profile_sequential_pipeline(file_paths: list[Path]) -> dict:
    """Profile each pipeline step to find the bottleneck."""
    timings = {}

    start = time.perf_counter()
    all_readings = []
    for path in file_paths:
        file_start = time.perf_counter()
        readings = read_csv_meters(path)  # I/O-bound: waiting for disk
        all_readings.extend(readings)
        timings[path.name] = time.perf_counter() - file_start
    timings['total_read'] = time.perf_counter() - start

    start = time.perf_counter()
    valid, rejected = validate_readings(all_readings)  # CPU-bound: regex, type conversion
    timings['validate'] = time.perf_counter() - start

    start = time.perf_counter()
    silver = transform_readings(valid)  # CPU-bound: normalization, derived fields
    timings['transform'] = time.perf_counter() - start

    start = time.perf_counter()
    write_parquet(silver, output_path)  # I/O-bound: writing to disk
    timings['write'] = time.perf_counter() - start

    return timings

# Results at CelticWind (1.2M meters, 8 files):
# total_read: 96s   (8 files x 12s each, sequential)
# validate:   45s   (regex + type coercion on 1.2M rows)
# transform:  18s   (normalization + derived fields)
# write:      12s   (Parquet compression + write)
# TOTAL:      171s  (2 min 51 sec)

The profiling reveals two bottleneck categories. The read step (96 seconds) is I/O-bound: the CPU waits for the disk to deliver bytes, and the 8 reads are independent of each other. The validate step (45 seconds) is CPU-bound: the CPU is fully utilized running regex patterns and type conversions on 1.2 million rows. These two categories require different concurrency solutions: threading for the I/O-bound reads (threads can wait for disk simultaneously) and multiprocessing for the CPU-bound validation (separate processes bypass the GIL to use multiple CPU cores). This lesson's profiling establishes the baseline; the remaining lessons teach the solutions.
I/O-Bound vs CPU-Bound

rows: CPU is idle, waiting for disk or network
File reads (96s), API calls, Parquet writes (12s)

blocks.content: The distinction between I/O-bound and CPU-bound determines the concurrency model. Using threading for CPU-bound work does not help (Python's GIL prevents threads from executing CPU-bound Python code simultaneously — lesson 2 explains why). Using multiprocessing for I/O-bound work works but is overkill (the overhead of spawning processes and serializing data exceeds the benefit when the bottleneck is disk speed, not CPU speed). At CelticWind, the Silver pipeline uses threading for the 96-second read phase and multiprocessing for the 45-second validation phase, cutting the total time from 171 seconds to approximately 68 seconds.

blocks.title: Profile before parallelizing
blocks.content: The instinct to parallelize everything is strong but wrong. CelticWind's write step (12 seconds) is I/O-bound but not worth parallelizing: the single Parquet write already saturates the disk's write bandwidth, and threading would not make it faster. The transform step (18 seconds) is CPU-bound but below the 60-second threshold where the engineering overhead of multiprocessing is justified (lesson 9). Profile first, identify the bottleneck, then apply the appropriate concurrency model — only to the steps that benefit.

sections.title: The GIL and Python's Concurrency Model

blocks.content: Python's Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) is a mutex that allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time. This means that a multi-threaded Python program with 4 threads running CPU-bound code does not use 4 CPU cores — it uses 1 core, with the threads taking turns. The GIL exists because CPython's memory management (reference counting) is not thread-safe: without the GIL, two threads incrementing and decrementing the same object's reference count simultaneously could corrupt memory. The GIL is a design trade-off: simpler memory management at the cost of single-threaded CPU execution.
What the GIL Allows and Prevents

blocks.title: GIL behavior for I/O-bound vs CPU-bound threads
blocks.content:
I/O-BOUND THREADS (file reads, API calls):
Thread 1: [read file A].............[process A]
Thread 2:     [read file B].............[process B]
Thread 3:         [read file C].............[process C]
              ↑ GIL released during I/O — threads run concurrently
              Total time ≈ max(read times) + serial processing

CPU-BOUND THREADS (validation, transformation):
Thread 1: [validate][    wait    ][validate][    wait    ]
Thread 2:     [    wait    ][validate][    wait    ][validate]
              ↑ GIL held during CPU work — threads take turns
              Total time ≈ sum(validate times) — NO speedup
blocks.caption: For I/O-bound operations, the GIL is released while the thread waits for I/O, allowing other threads to run simultaneously. For CPU-bound operations, the GIL is held while the thread executes Python bytecode, forcing threads to take turns and providing no speedup over sequential execution.

blocks.content: The critical insight: the GIL is released during I/O operations (file reads, network calls, sleep). When Thread 1 calls open(path).read(), the GIL is released while the operating system reads bytes from disk. During this wait, Thread 2 can acquire the GIL and start its own file read. Both threads are 'waiting for I/O' simultaneously — true concurrency for the waiting phase. This is why threading works for I/O-bound tasks: the threads overlap their waiting time, not their computing time. For CelticWind's 8 file reads, threading allows all 8 disk reads to proceed simultaneously, reducing the total from 96 seconds (8 × 12s) to approximately 15 seconds (the longest single read plus overhead).
The Three Concurrency Models

rows: Threads share the GIL; released during I/O
I/O-bound: file reads, API calls, database queries (< 20 concurrent tasks)
Each process has its own GIL; true parallelism
CPU-bound: validation, transformation, compression (2-8 parallel tasks)
Single thread, cooperative scheduling; GIL irrelevant

blocks.content: Threading uses multiple threads sharing one GIL — effective for I/O-bound tasks where the GIL is released during waiting. Multiprocessing uses multiple processes, each with its own Python interpreter and GIL — effective for CPU-bound tasks where true parallel computation is needed. asyncio uses a single thread with cooperative scheduling — effective for high-concurrency I/O where managing thousands of threads would be wasteful. At CelticWind, the daily pipeline uses threading (lesson 3) for reading 8 files, multiprocessing (lesson 5) for validating 1.2M rows, and asyncio (lesson 6) for the historical backfill that fetches 365 days of prices concurrently.

blocks.title: The GIL may change in Python 3.13+
blocks.content: PEP 703 proposes making the GIL optional in CPython (the 'free-threaded' build). As of Python 3.13, the no-GIL build is experimental. If it becomes the default, threading will work for CPU-bound tasks too. This module teaches the current model (GIL-constrained threading) because it is the production reality for Python 3.12 and the patterns (choosing between threading and multiprocessing based on I/O vs CPU) remain good engineering discipline regardless of GIL status.

The concurrent.futures module provides ThreadPoolExecutor — a managed pool of worker threads that execute callables concurrently and collect their results. Instead of reading 8 files sequentially in a for loop, the pipeline submits 8 read tasks to the pool and the pool executes them concurrently. The main thread waits for all tasks to complete and collects the results. The API is intentionally simple: submit a function and its arguments, get a Future object, and call result() to retrieve the return value (or re-raise any exception the function threw).
Concurrent File Reading

from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed
from pathlib import Path
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def read_files_concurrently(
    file_paths: list[Path],
    reader: DataReader,
    max_workers: int = 4,
) -> list[dict]:
    """Read multiple files concurrently using a thread pool.

    Returns all records from all files combined.
    Raises the first exception from any failed read.
    """
    all_records = []

    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=max_workers) as pool:
        # Submit all read tasks
        future_to_path = {
            pool.submit(reader.read, path): path
            for path in file_paths
        }

        # Collect results as they complete
        for future in as_completed(future_to_path):
            path = future_to_path[future]
            try:
                records = future.result()
                all_records.extend(records)
                logger.info('Read %d records from %s', len(records), path.name)
            except Exception as e:
                logger.error('Failed to read %s: %s', path.name, e)
                raise

    return all_records

# Usage: read 8 regional files with 4 concurrent threads
readings = read_files_concurrently(
    file_paths=sorted(bronze_dir.glob('*.csv')),
    reader=CsvReader(encoding='utf-8'),
    max_workers=4,
)

The ThreadPoolExecutor manages a pool of 4 worker threads. The pool.submit(reader.read, path) call schedules the read operation on a worker thread and returns a Future immediately — without waiting for the read to complete. The as_completed() function yields futures as they finish, regardless of submission order (the fastest file finishes first). The future.result() call retrieves the return value or re-raises the exception. At CelticWind, 4 workers is optimal for 8 files on a 4-core server: each core handles one active disk read, and the OS overlaps I/O operations across the 4 read streams.
Thread Safety Considerations

The read_files_concurrently function is thread-safe because each thread operates on independent data: thread 1 reads file A into its own list, thread 2 reads file B into its own list. The results are collected in the main thread by extending all_records — but this happens sequentially (one future at a time in the for loop), so no two threads modify all_records simultaneously. The critical rule: threads should not share mutable state. If two threads append to the same list concurrently, Python's list is not thread-safe for concurrent appends (items can be lost or the list can be corrupted). The as_completed pattern avoids this by collecting results sequentially in the main thread.
Choosing max_workers

The max_workers parameter controls how many threads run simultaneously. For file I/O, the optimal value depends on the storage subsystem: a local SSD supports 4-8 concurrent reads efficiently; a network-attached storage or cloud blob supports 16-32 concurrent reads. Too few workers underutilize the I/O bandwidth; too many workers cause contention (threads compete for the same disk head or network bandwidth). At CelticWind, the default is 4 for local SSD and 8 for Azure Blob Storage — configured in the pipeline's YAML file so the value can be tuned per environment without code changes.

blocks.title: Use the context manager pattern for thread pools
blocks.content: The with ThreadPoolExecutor() as pool: pattern ensures the pool is shut down cleanly after all tasks complete — even if an exception occurs. Without the context manager, a forgotten pool.shutdown() leaves worker threads running after the pipeline exits, consuming resources and potentially holding file locks. At CelticWind, every thread pool and process pool uses the context manager pattern.

CelticWind's pipeline fetches weather forecasts from the Met Éireann API for 8 sub-regions. Each API call takes 2-5 seconds (network latency + server processing). Sequential fetching: 8 × 3.5 seconds average = 28 seconds. Concurrent fetching with 4 threads: approximately 8 seconds (2 batches of 4 concurrent calls). The pattern is identical to concurrent file reads — submit tasks to a ThreadPoolExecutor and collect results — but API calls add two concerns that file reads do not: rate limiting (the API allows 60 requests per minute) and per-call error isolation (one station's 503 error should not cancel the other 7 stations' successful responses).
Rate-Limited Concurrent Fetching

from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed
from threading import Semaphore
import time
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def fetch_weather_concurrently(
    station_ids: list[str],
    client: WeatherClient,
    max_concurrent: int = 4,
    min_interval: float = 1.0,
) -> dict[str, list[dict]]:
    """Fetch weather for multiple stations concurrently with rate limiting.

    Returns a dict mapping station_id to forecast data.
    Stations that fail are logged and skipped (partial success is acceptable).
    """
    semaphore = Semaphore(max_concurrent)
    results = {}
    errors = {}

    def rate_limited_fetch(station_id: str) -> tuple[str, list[dict]]:
        with semaphore:  # limits concurrent calls
            time.sleep(min_interval)  # space calls 1 second apart
            forecasts = client.get_forecast(station_id)
            return station_id, forecasts

    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=max_concurrent) as pool:
        futures = {
            pool.submit(rate_limited_fetch, sid): sid
            for sid in station_ids
        }
        for future in as_completed(futures):
            station_id = futures[future]
            try:
                sid, forecasts = future.result()
                results[sid] = forecasts
                logger.info('Fetched %d forecasts for %s', len(forecasts), sid)
            except Exception as e:
                errors[station_id] = str(e)
                logger.warning('Failed to fetch %s: %s', station_id, e)

    if errors:
        logger.warning('Failed stations: %s', list(errors.keys()))
    return results

The Semaphore(max_concurrent) limits how many threads can execute the API call simultaneously — even if the pool has more workers available. The time.sleep(min_interval) spaces calls at least 1 second apart, staying within the API's 60 requests/minute limit. The error handling per future isolates failures: if the GALWAY-COAST station returns a 503, the function logs the error and continues collecting results from the other 7 stations. The results dictionary contains successful responses; the errors dictionary contains failures. At CelticWind, partial success is acceptable for weather data (forecasts are optional enrichment, not required for billing), so a failed station does not fail the pipeline.
Error Isolation vs Fail-Fast

left.label: Error isolation (weather forecasts)
points: One failed station does not cancel others
Partial results are acceptable (weather enrichment is optional)
Errors are logged and collected; pipeline continues
Use for: enrichment data, non-critical sources

right.label: Fail-fast (meter readings)
points: One failed file fails the entire batch
Partial results are not acceptable (every meter must be processed)
First error is re-raised; pipeline stops immediately
Use for: primary data, billing-critical sources

blocks.content: The choice between error isolation and fail-fast depends on whether partial results are acceptable. CelticWind's weather enrichment uses error isolation because a missing forecast for one station does not invalidate the other 7 stations' data. CelticWind's meter readings use fail-fast because a missing file means a sub-region's billing data is absent — the pipeline should not produce a partial billing summary that undercounts consumption. The concurrency infrastructure (ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed) is identical for both; the error handling strategy (log-and-continue vs raise-immediately) is the only difference.

blocks.title: Rate limiting with threads requires coordination
blocks.content: Without the Semaphore, all 8 threads would start their API calls simultaneously, potentially sending 8 requests in under 1 second — far exceeding the 60/minute limit. The Semaphore limits concurrency, and the time.sleep() spaces calls. Together, they ensure the pipeline stays within the rate limit. If the API returns 429 (rate limited), the retry logic from Module 7 junior handles the recovery. The concurrent code's job is to prevent the 429 from happening; the retry logic's job is to recover if it happens anyway.

CelticWind's validation step processes 1.2 million meter readings: parsing daily_kw as a float, running regex patterns against meter_id, checking region membership in a set, and validating date formats. This work is CPU-bound — every cycle is spent on Python computation, not waiting for I/O. Threading provides no speedup because the GIL prevents threads from executing Python bytecode in parallel. Multiprocessing bypasses the GIL entirely: each process has its own Python interpreter, its own GIL, and its own CPU core. Four processes validating 300,000 readings each run truly in parallel, reducing the validation time from 45 seconds to approximately 13 seconds.
ProcessPoolExecutor for Validation

from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def validate_chunk(chunk: list[dict]) -> tuple[list[dict], list[dict]]:
    """Validate a chunk of readings. Must be a top-level function (picklable)."""
    valid, rejected = [], []
    for record in chunk:
        try:
            model = MeterReadingModel(**record)
            valid.append(model.model_dump())
        except ValidationError as e:
            rejected.append({**record, '_reject_reason': str(e)})
    return valid, rejected

def validate_parallel(
    readings: list[dict],
    num_workers: int = 4,
    chunk_size: int = 50_000,
) -> tuple[list[dict], list[dict]]:
    """Validate readings in parallel using multiple processes."""
    # Split into chunks
    chunks = [
        readings[i:i + chunk_size]
        for i in range(0, len(readings), chunk_size)
    ]
    logger.info('Validating %d readings in %d chunks across %d workers',
                len(readings), len(chunks), num_workers)

    all_valid, all_rejected = [], []
    with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=num_workers) as pool:
        for valid, rejected in pool.map(validate_chunk, chunks):
            all_valid.extend(valid)
            all_rejected.extend(rejected)

    logger.info('Validation complete: %d valid, %d rejected',
                len(all_valid), len(all_rejected))
    return all_valid, all_rejected

The function splits 1.2 million readings into chunks of 50,000 and distributes them across 4 worker processes. Each process runs validate_chunk independently — with its own Python interpreter, its own GIL, and its own CPU core. The pool.map() call distributes chunks to workers and collects results in order. The critical constraint: validate_chunk must be a top-level function (not a lambda, not a method, not a nested function) because multiprocessing serializes functions using pickle, which cannot serialize closures or local functions.
The Serialization Overhead

Multiprocessing sends data between processes by serializing (pickling) it. Each chunk of 50,000 dictionaries must be serialized in the main process, transferred to the worker process, and deserialized there. For CelticWind's 1.2M readings, the serialization overhead is approximately 3 seconds (4 chunks × 0.75 seconds each). The validation itself takes 11 seconds across 4 workers (45 seconds / 4, approximately). Total with overhead: 14 seconds — still a 69% improvement over the 45-second sequential version. But if the chunks were smaller (1,000 readings each), the serialization overhead would dominate: 1,200 chunks × 0.05 seconds each = 60 seconds of serialization alone, making multiprocessing slower than sequential.
Choosing chunk_size and num_workers

The chunk_size trades serialization overhead against parallelism granularity. Large chunks (300,000 each for 4 workers) minimize serialization overhead but create 4 tasks — if one chunk finishes faster than the others, its worker sits idle. Smaller chunks (50,000 each = 24 tasks for 4 workers) balance the load better — workers pick up new chunks as they finish — but have more serialization overhead. At CelticWind, 50,000 rows per chunk is the sweet spot: the serialization overhead is 3% of the total time, and the load balancing ensures all 4 workers stay busy throughout. The num_workers defaults to the number of CPU cores minus 1 (leaving one core for the main process and the OS).

blocks.title: Multiprocessing cannot share mutable state safely
blocks.content: Each worker process has its own memory space. A global variable modified in one worker is invisible to others. A shared list or dictionary requires explicit inter-process communication (multiprocessing.Manager, shared memory), which adds complexity and serialization overhead. At CelticWind, workers receive their chunk as an argument and return their results — no shared state, no communication between workers. If your design requires shared state between workers, reconsider whether multiprocessing is the right tool.

CelticWind's historical backfill job fetches 365 days of energy prices from the EPEX SPOT API. With threading (max 4 concurrent calls), the backfill takes approximately 365 / 4 × 3.5 seconds = 320 seconds (5.3 minutes). The API can handle 60 requests per minute, which means the pipeline could make 10 concurrent calls at 6-second intervals. But ThreadPoolExecutor with 10 threads means 10 OS threads — each consuming memory and kernel resources. For higher concurrency (50-1000 concurrent calls to multiple APIs), thread overhead becomes significant. asyncio provides an alternative: a single thread that manages thousands of concurrent I/O operations using cooperative scheduling, with negligible per-operation overhead.
async/await Basics

import asyncio
import aiohttp
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

async def fetch_price(session: aiohttp.ClientSession, date: str, api_key: str) -> dict:
    """Fetch prices for a single date asynchronously."""
    url = 'https://api.epexspot.com/v1/prices'
    params = {'date': date, 'market_zone': 'IE'}
    headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {api_key}'}

    async with session.get(url, params=params, headers=headers, timeout=30) as resp:
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = await resp.json()
        return {'date': date, 'prices': data['prices']}

async def backfill_prices(
    dates: list[str],
    api_key: str,
    max_concurrent: int = 10,
) -> list[dict]:
    """Fetch prices for multiple dates with concurrency limit."""
    semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent)
    results = []

    async def limited_fetch(session, date):
        async with semaphore:
            return await fetch_price(session, date, api_key)

    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [limited_fetch(session, d) for d in dates]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)

    return [r for r in results if not isinstance(r, Exception)]

# Run the backfill
prices = asyncio.run(backfill_prices(dates=['2025-01-01', '2025-01-02', ...], api_key=key))

The async def keyword defines a coroutine — a function that can pause execution (at await points) to let other coroutines run. await resp.json() pauses the current coroutine while the HTTP response is being received, allowing other coroutines to make their own HTTP requests during the wait. asyncio.gather() runs multiple coroutines concurrently: all 365 date-fetch coroutines are started, and the semaphore limits how many are actively waiting for HTTP responses at any moment (10 concurrent). The result: 365 API calls complete in approximately 365 / 10 × 3.5 seconds = 128 seconds, using a single thread.
asyncio vs Threading for API Calls

For CelticWind's daily pipeline (8 concurrent weather API calls), threading is simpler and sufficient — 8 threads have negligible overhead. For the historical backfill (365 concurrent price API calls), asyncio is more efficient: 365 OS threads would consume 365 MB of stack memory and create scheduling overhead, while asyncio handles 365 coroutines in under 1 MB with a single thread. The threshold at CelticWind: threading for fewer than 20 concurrent I/O operations, asyncio for 20 or more. The trade-off is complexity: asyncio requires async/await syntax throughout the call chain, a compatible HTTP library (aiohttp instead of requests), and an event loop (asyncio.run).

blocks.title: return_exceptions=True prevents one failure from canceling all tasks
blocks.content: By default, asyncio.gather() cancels all remaining tasks when any task raises an exception. With return_exceptions=True, exceptions are returned as values in the result list instead of being raised. The list comprehension [r for r in results if not isinstance(r, Exception)] filters out the failed dates. This matches the error-isolation pattern from lesson 4: failed dates are logged and skipped, successful dates are collected.

Concurrent code makes error handling harder for three reasons. First, exceptions in worker threads and processes are invisible to the main thread unless explicitly checked — a thread that crashes silently loses its work without any notification. Second, the order of execution is non-deterministic: the same error may appear on different threads in different runs, making reproduction difficult. Third, cleanup after partial failure is complex: if 3 of 8 file reads succeed and the 4th fails, the pipeline must decide whether to use the 3 successful results (partial success) or discard everything (fail-fast). This lesson covers the patterns for handling all three challenges.
Collecting Successes and Failures

The ConcurrentResult dataclass collects both successes and failures as structured data. The caller inspects the result and decides the policy: if result.failure_count > 0 and the pipeline requires all files, it raises an error. If partial success is acceptable, it processes result.all_records and logs the failures. This separation of collection from policy is critical: the concurrent infrastructure should collect results neutrally; the pipeline should decide what failures mean. At CelticWind, the meter reading pipeline requires all 8 files (fails if any are missing); the weather enrichment pipeline accepts partial results.
The Circuit Breaker Pattern

When making concurrent API calls, a server outage can cause all concurrent calls to fail simultaneously — producing N error logs, N retry attempts, and N timeout waits. The circuit breaker pattern detects consecutive failures and stops making new calls until the server recovers. At CelticWind, the circuit breaker tracks the last 10 API call results. If 5 consecutive calls fail, the breaker 'opens' and all subsequent calls return a cached fallback or a SourceUnavailableError immediately — without making the HTTP request. After a 60-second cooldown, the breaker allows one 'probe' call. If the probe succeeds, the breaker 'closes' and normal calls resume. This prevents a downed API from causing minutes of timeout waits across concurrent threads.

blocks.title: Test concurrent error handling with deterministic failures
blocks.content: The hardest part of concurrent error handling is testing it. In production, errors are non-deterministic: the failing thread varies between runs. In tests, use dependency injection (Module 1) to inject a reader that fails deterministically: FailingReader(fail_on=['CORK-EAST.csv']) raises FileNotFoundError for one specific file and succeeds for all others. This lets the test verify that the pipeline collects 7 successes and 1 failure, regardless of thread execution order.

Lessons 3-6 presented three concurrency models: threading, multiprocessing, and asyncio. Choosing the wrong model wastes engineering effort and may provide no speedup at all. Threading for CPU-bound work provides zero benefit because of the GIL. Multiprocessing for I/O-bound work provides benefit but at higher overhead than threading (process creation, data serialization across process boundaries). asyncio for 4 concurrent operations is more complex than threading with no meaningful benefit at that scale. This lesson provides the decision framework that CelticWind uses to choose the right model for each pipeline step.
The Decision Framework

rows: Is the step I/O-bound or CPU-bound?
Is the step I/O-bound or CPU-bound?
Is the step I/O-bound or CPU-bound?
Does the step take < 60 seconds sequentially?
Keep it sequential — concurrency overhead exceeds the benefit
Does the step involve shared mutable state?
Redesign to eliminate shared state before parallelizing

blocks.content: The framework starts with the bottleneck category (I/O vs CPU), then considers the scale (number of concurrent operations or data volume), and finally applies the 60-second threshold. A step that takes 45 seconds sequentially but 40 seconds with threading (5 seconds of overhead eats most of the benefit) is not worth parallelizing. A step that takes 96 seconds sequentially but 15 seconds with threading (81 seconds saved) is clearly worth the added complexity. At CelticWind, the decision is documented in the pipeline's YAML configuration as a comment: # concurrent: threading, reason: 8 file reads, 96s sequential, 15s concurrent.
CelticWind's Concurrency Map

The map shows that 3 of 6 steps benefit from concurrency (file reads, weather fetches, validation), 2 are below the 60-second threshold (transform, write), and 1 uses asyncio for high concurrency (backfill). The total pipeline time drops from 519 seconds (8.6 minutes) to 195 seconds (3.3 minutes) — a 62% reduction. The engineering cost: 3 functions were changed from sequential to concurrent. The 3 unchanged functions (transform, write, and the pipeline orchestration) remain simple, sequential code that is easy to test and debug.

blocks.title: Concurrency is a targeted optimization, not an architecture
blocks.content: The pipeline's architecture (protocols, DI, composition from Module 1) is unchanged by concurrency. The concurrent file reader satisfies the same DataReader protocol as the sequential one. The multiprocessing validator produces the same (valid, rejected) tuple as the sequential one. Concurrency is applied to specific steps that cross the benefit threshold — not to the entire pipeline. This targeted approach keeps the pipeline readable: most of the code is sequential, and the concurrent sections are isolated in well-tested functions.

Concurrency adds complexity at every level: the code is harder to read (futures, semaphores, async/await), harder to debug (non-deterministic execution order, invisible exceptions), harder to test (race conditions, timing dependencies), and harder to reason about (which thread modified which data, when). Every concurrent pipeline is harder to maintain than its sequential equivalent. The question is not 'can this step be parallelized?' but 'does the speedup justify the complexity?' At CelticWind, 3 of the 12 production pipelines use concurrency. The other 9 run sequentially because their total runtime is under the SLA and the engineering team determined that the concurrency complexity was not justified.
The Anti-Patterns

rows: Most steps are fast enough sequentially; concurrency overhead is wasted
The transform step (18s) was parallelized, adding 200 lines of code for a 4-second improvement
The GIL prevents speedup; the code runs at sequential speed with thread overhead
An engineer threaded the validation step before learning about the GIL — no speedup, harder to debug
Race conditions produce non-deterministic bugs that are nearly impossible to reproduce
Two threads appending to a shared list produced occasional missing rows — detected only under high load
async/await syntax infects the entire call chain for a negligible speedup over threading
The weather fetch was rewritten with asyncio for 8 calls — reverted to threading because the code tripled in complexity

blocks.content: Each anti-pattern violates the same principle: the concurrency complexity exceeded the runtime benefit. CelticWind's 60-second threshold exists because of these experiences. The transform step was parallelized (saving 4 seconds), then de-parallelized 3 months later when the team spent 2 days debugging a serialization issue in the multiprocessing worker. The weather fetch was rewritten with asyncio (saving 2 seconds over threading), then reverted when the async code made the function signature incompatible with the synchronous pipeline framework. The cost of concurrency is paid in engineering time; the benefit is measured in runtime seconds.
The CelticWind Decision Policy

Parallelize a step only when all of the following conditions are met: the step takes more than 60 seconds sequentially (the minimum threshold where concurrency benefit justifies complexity), the step is the pipeline's bottleneck (parallelizing a non-bottleneck step does not reduce total runtime), the step's work is independent (no shared mutable state, no ordering dependencies), and the team has tested the concurrent version under realistic load (not just with 10 rows in development). If any condition is not met, the step stays sequential. At CelticWind, this policy is documented in the engineering guidelines and reviewed during pipeline architecture discussions.

blocks.title: Sequential code is not a failure — it is a feature
blocks.content: A pipeline that runs in 3 minutes within a 30-minute SLA using purely sequential code is not a performance problem — it is a maintenance advantage. Every line of sequential code is debuggable, testable, and comprehensible by the newest team member. The 27 minutes of SLA headroom provide safety margin for data growth, infrastructure slowdowns, and occasional retries. Adding concurrency to save 2 minutes burns the headroom faster than the data grows. At CelticWind, the default is sequential; concurrency requires justification.

This module taught the three concurrency models for Python data pipelines: threading for I/O-bound tasks with low concurrency (parallel file reads, concurrent API calls with rate limiting), multiprocessing for CPU-bound tasks (parallel validation across separate processes), and asyncio for high-concurrency I/O (hundreds of concurrent API calls for backfills). It also taught the discipline of concurrency: profile before parallelizing, choose the model based on I/O vs CPU, handle errors with structured result collection, and apply the 60-second threshold before adding complexity. This lesson assembles the patterns into CelticWind's production concurrent pipeline.
The Concurrent Pipeline Architecture

import logging
from pathlib import Path
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, ProcessPoolExecutor

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class ConcurrentSilverPipeline:
    """Silver pipeline with targeted concurrency for bottleneck steps."""

    def __init__(self, reader: DataReader, validator: DataValidator,
                 transformer: DataTransformer, silver_repo: DataRepository,
                 io_workers: int = 4, cpu_workers: int = 4):
        self.reader = reader
        self.validator = validator
        self.transformer = transformer
        self.silver_repo = silver_repo
        self.io_workers = io_workers
        self.cpu_workers = cpu_workers

    def run(self, file_paths: list[Path], date: str) -> dict:
        # Step 1: Concurrent file reads (threading — I/O-bound)
        logger.info('Reading %d files with %d I/O workers', len(file_paths), self.io_workers)
        readings = self._read_concurrent(file_paths)

        # Step 2: Parallel validation (multiprocessing — CPU-bound)
        logger.info('Validating %d readings with %d CPU workers', len(readings), self.cpu_workers)
        valid, rejected = self._validate_parallel(readings)

        # Step 3: Sequential transform (below 60s threshold)
        silver = self.transformer.transform(valid)

        # Step 4: Sequential write (single I/O stream)
        self.silver_repo.save(silver, f'meters/{date}')

        return {'read': len(readings), 'valid': len(valid), 'rejected': len(rejected)}

    def _read_concurrent(self, paths: list[Path]) -> list[dict]:
        all_records = []
        with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=self.io_workers) as pool:
            futures = {pool.submit(self.reader.read, p): p for p in paths}
            for future in futures:
                all_records.extend(future.result())
        return all_records

    def _validate_parallel(self, readings: list[dict]) -> tuple[list[dict], list[dict]]:
        chunk_size = max(len(readings) // (self.cpu_workers * 2), 10_000)
        chunks = [readings[i:i+chunk_size] for i in range(0, len(readings), chunk_size)]
        all_valid, all_rejected = [], []
        with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=self.cpu_workers) as pool:
            for valid, rejected in pool.map(validate_chunk, chunks):
                all_valid.extend(valid)
                all_rejected.extend(rejected)
        return all_valid, all_rejected

The pipeline uses threading for the I/O-bound read step and multiprocessing for the CPU-bound validation step. The transform and write steps remain sequential (below the 60-second threshold). The architecture from Module 1 (protocols, DI) is preserved: the ConcurrentSilverPipeline receives its reader, validator, transformer, and repository through the constructor. The concurrency is internal to the pipeline — the factory function and the test suite interact with the same interface as the sequential version. At CelticWind, the sequential and concurrent pipelines coexist: the daily pipeline uses ConcurrentSilverPipeline; the development environment uses the sequential SilverPipeline for simpler debugging.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 3 addresses the next scalability constraint: memory. CelticWind's pipeline loads 1.2M readings into a list[dict] — consuming 2.4 GB of RAM. The concurrent pipeline from this module makes the problem worse: the ThreadPoolExecutor reads 8 files concurrently, producing 8 lists that are merged into one 2.4 GB list before validation begins. Module 3 teaches chunked processing (process data in 50K-row batches without loading the full dataset), Polars for memory-efficient analytical transforms (400 MB instead of 2.4 GB), and pyarrow for zero-copy data transfer between stages.

modules.title: Memory-Efficient Data Processing
modules.description: Process million-row datasets without exhausting memory: chunked processing pipelines, Polars for analytical transforms, memory profiling, and the architecture that keeps memory usage constant regardless of dataset size.

blocks.content: CelticWind's junior-level pipeline loads all meter readings into a list[dict] before processing them. At 400,000 meters, this consumes approximately 600 MB — comfortable on a developer's laptop with 16 GB of RAM. At the mid-level scale of 1.2 million meters, the same pattern consumes approximately 2.4 GB. The production server has 4 GB of RAM, shared with the operating system, the Python runtime, and the logging infrastructure. The pipeline crashes with MemoryError at 03:12 AM, having consumed 3.8 GB before the OS kills it. The morning engineer sees a cryptic 'Killed' message in the logs and spends 2 hours diagnosing what turns out to be a straightforward problem: the data no longer fits in memory using the junior-level approach.
Mapping the Memory Budget

import tracemalloc

tracemalloc.start()

# Step 1: Read 1.2M readings into list[dict]
readings = read_csv_meters(bronze_path)  # 1.2M rows
snapshot_after_read = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()

# Step 2: Validate (creates a second list)
valid, rejected = validate_readings(readings)
snapshot_after_validate = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()

# Step 3: Transform (creates a third list)
silver = transform_readings(valid)
snapshot_after_transform = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()

# Memory at each step:
# After read:      2,400 MB (1.2M dicts at ~2 KB each)
# After validate:  3,600 MB (original list + valid list + rejected list)
# After transform: 4,800 MB (all previous + silver list) — CRASH

for stat in snapshot_after_transform.statistics('lineno')[:5]:
    print(stat)

The profiling reveals the root cause: each pipeline step creates a new list without releasing the previous one. The read step allocates 2.4 GB. The validate step creates valid and rejected lists (1.2 GB additional). The transform step creates the Silver list (1.2 GB additional). At peak, 4.8 GB is allocated simultaneously — exceeding the 4 GB server capacity. The junior-level pattern of 'load everything, then process' works when the dataset fits comfortably in memory with room for intermediate copies. At 1.2 million rows, it does not. This module teaches three solutions: chunked processing (process data in batches), Polars (columnar in-memory representation that uses 5x less memory), and streaming architecture (process one record at a time).
The Three Solutions

rows: Minimal: one record at a time

blocks.title: Memory is the constraint that separates junior from mid-level Python
blocks.content: At junior-level scale (400K rows), memory is free — load everything, process it, write it. At mid-level scale (1.2M rows), memory is a budget that the pipeline must manage. The shift from 'load and process' to 'chunk and stream' is the defining architectural change of mid-level Python data engineering. Every technique in this module exists because the data outgrew the available memory, and the pipeline must adapt without upgrading the hardware.

Before optimizing memory, you must measure it. Python's tracemalloc module (standard library since 3.4) tracks every memory allocation and can report where memory is being used, which objects consume the most, and how memory grows across pipeline steps. The measure-first principle from Module 2's profiling workflow (lesson 1) applies here: without measurement, engineers guess at the memory bottleneck and often guess wrong. CelticWind's team initially assumed the Parquet write was the memory hog (Parquet buffers data for compression). tracemalloc revealed that 80% of the memory was consumed by the list[dict] construction in the read step — the write step used only 200 MB.
Taking Memory Snapshots

import tracemalloc
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def measure_memory_at_step(step_name: str) -> None:
    """Log the current memory usage and top allocators."""
    snapshot = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()
    stats = snapshot.statistics('lineno')

    total_mb = sum(s.size for s in stats) / (1024 * 1024)
    logger.info('Memory after %s: %.1f MB', step_name, total_mb)

    # Top 3 allocators by size
    for stat in stats[:3]:
        logger.debug('  %s', stat)

def run_pipeline_with_memory_tracking(config: dict) -> dict:
    """Run the Silver pipeline with per-step memory measurement."""
    tracemalloc.start()

    readings = read_csv_meters(config['bronze_path'])
    measure_memory_at_step('read')       # 2,400 MB

    valid, rejected = validate_readings(readings)
    del readings  # free the original list — no longer needed
    measure_memory_at_step('validate')   # 1,200 MB (freed 2,400, allocated 1,200)

    silver = transform_readings(valid)
    del valid  # free the valid list
    measure_memory_at_step('transform')  # 1,200 MB

    write_parquet(silver, config['silver_path'])
    del silver
    measure_memory_at_step('write')      # ~50 MB (all data lists freed)

    tracemalloc.stop()
    return {'peak_mb': tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()[1] / (1024 * 1024)}

The del statements explicitly free the intermediate lists after they are no longer needed. Without del readings after the validate step, the original 2.4 GB list stays in memory alongside the 1.2 GB valid list — totaling 3.6 GB. With del readings, the garbage collector reclaims the 2.4 GB, and the validate step's peak is 1.2 GB. This explicit memory management — deleting intermediate results as soon as they are consumed — reduces the peak memory from 4.8 GB to 2.4 GB without changing the pipeline logic. It is a simple optimization, but at CelticWind it was the difference between a pipeline that crashes and one that completes.
Comparing Memory Before and After Optimization

import tracemalloc

def compare_memory(label: str, func, *args):
    """Measure peak memory usage of a function call."""
    tracemalloc.start()
    result = func(*args)
    current, peak = tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()
    tracemalloc.stop()
    print(f'{label}: peak={peak / 1024 / 1024:.1f} MB')
    return result

# Compare list[dict] vs chunked processing
compare_memory('list[dict]', process_all_at_once, bronze_path)
# list[dict]: peak=2400.0 MB

compare_memory('chunked', process_in_chunks, bronze_path, chunk_size=50000)
# chunked: peak=120.0 MB

The compare_memory helper measures the peak memory of any function call. Comparing the list[dict] approach (2.4 GB peak) against the chunked approach (120 MB peak) quantifies the improvement: 20x less memory for the same pipeline logic. At CelticWind, this comparison function is used during pipeline development to verify that new code does not regress the memory profile. The peak memory measurement is also included in the structured validation report (Module 8 junior, lesson 9) as memory_peak_mb — a metric the monitoring dashboard tracks alongside runtime and rejection rate.

blocks.title: Use del for large intermediate results, not for every variable
blocks.content: Explicit del is useful when a large object (a million-row list) is no longer needed and the next step will allocate another large object. It is not useful for small objects (a configuration dictionary, a file path) where the garbage collector handles cleanup efficiently. At CelticWind, the convention is: use del for any list or DataFrame that exceeds 100 MB and is not needed after the current step. Do not sprinkle del throughout the code for small objects — it adds noise without benefit.

The junior-level track (Module 4, lesson 6) introduced generators that yield records one at a time. Chunked processing extends this pattern to batches: instead of loading all 1.2 million readings into memory and processing them at once, the pipeline loads 50,000 readings at a time, processes the batch, writes the output, and moves to the next batch. At any moment, only 50,000 readings (approximately 100 MB) are in memory — regardless of whether the total dataset is 1.2 million or 12 million rows. The memory usage is constant, bounded by the chunk size, not by the dataset size.
The Chunked Pipeline Pattern

import csv
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Iterator
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def read_csv_in_chunks(path: Path, chunk_size: int = 50_000) -> Iterator[list[dict]]:
    """Yield chunks of rows from a CSV file."""
    with open(path, 'r', encoding='utf-8', newline='') as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        chunk = []
        for row in reader:
            chunk.append(row)
            if len(chunk) >= chunk_size:
                yield chunk
                chunk = []
        if chunk:  # yield the remaining partial chunk
            yield chunk

def run_chunked_pipeline(config: dict) -> dict:
    """Process meter readings in chunks — constant memory usage."""
    total_valid = 0
    total_rejected = 0
    chunk_num = 0

    for chunk in read_csv_in_chunks(Path(config['bronze_path']), chunk_size=50_000):
        chunk_num += 1
        valid, rejected = validate_readings(chunk)
        silver = transform_readings(valid)
        append_parquet(silver, Path(config['silver_path']))  # append mode

        total_valid += len(valid)
        total_rejected += len(rejected)
        logger.info('Chunk %d: %d valid, %d rejected', chunk_num, len(valid), len(rejected))
        # chunk, valid, rejected, silver are garbage collected at the end of this iteration

    return {'valid': total_valid, 'rejected': total_rejected, 'chunks': chunk_num}

The read_csv_in_chunks generator yields lists of 50,000 dictionaries. The pipeline loop processes each chunk independently: validate, transform, and append to the output Parquet file. At the end of each loop iteration, the chunk, valid, rejected, and silver variables go out of scope, and the garbage collector reclaims their memory before the next chunk is loaded. The peak memory is 2 × chunk_size × 2 KB (the current chunk plus the transformed output) — approximately 200 MB for 50,000-row chunks, regardless of total dataset size.
Appending vs Overwriting

The chunked pipeline writes output incrementally: each chunk is appended to the output Parquet file. This differs from the junior-level pattern of writing the complete result at once. The append pattern requires either a Parquet library that supports appending to an existing file (pyarrow's write_to_dataset with existing_data_behavior='overwrite_or_ignore') or writing one Parquet file per chunk (data/silver/meters/chunk_001.parquet, chunk_002.parquet). At CelticWind, the pipeline writes one file per chunk into a date-partitioned directory, and a post-processing OPTIMIZE step merges the chunk files into a single compacted file — the same pattern that Spark and Delta Lake use for streaming micro-batch output.
Chunk Size Selection

The chunk size is a trade-off between memory usage and processing overhead. Small chunks (1,000 rows) use minimal memory but incur overhead per chunk: opening/closing file handles, Parquet footer writes, and function call overhead for validate and transform. Large chunks (500,000 rows) reduce overhead but use more memory. At CelticWind, the optimal chunk size was determined empirically: profiling the pipeline at chunk sizes of 10K, 50K, 100K, and 500K showed that 50K rows produced the best balance — 100 MB of memory per chunk, 24 chunks for 1.2M rows, and processing overhead under 5% of total runtime. The chunk size is configurable in the YAML file (config['processing']['chunk_size']) so it can be tuned per environment.

blocks.title: Aggregations across chunks require a merge step
blocks.content: Chunked processing works naturally for per-record operations (validation, transformation, enrichment) where each chunk's output is independent. Aggregations (regional totals, daily summaries) require combining results across chunks: each chunk produces a partial aggregation, and a final merge step combines the partial results. At CelticWind, the chunked pipeline handles per-record Silver processing in chunks and performs Gold aggregation as a separate step that reads the completed Silver Parquet files. The aggregation step uses Polars (lesson 4) for memory-efficient analytical processing.

The chunked pipeline from lesson 3 processes data in batches using list[dict] — Python dictionaries that store each field name per row. Polars is a DataFrame library that stores data in a columnar format using Apache Arrow as its memory backend. Columnar storage means all values of one column are stored contiguously in memory: all 1.2 million daily_kw values are in one array, all 1.2 million meter_id values are in another. This layout is dramatically more memory-efficient than list[dict] (400 MB vs 2.4 GB for 1.2M rows) and dramatically faster for column-level operations (sum, filter, group-by) because the CPU reads contiguous memory instead of chasing pointers between dictionary objects.
Reading Data with Polars

import polars as pl

# Read a CSV file into a Polars DataFrame
df = pl.read_csv(
    'data/bronze/meters_20250115.csv',
    dtypes={'meter_id': pl.Utf8, 'daily_kw': pl.Float64, 'date': pl.Utf8,
            'region': pl.Utf8, 'customer_type': pl.Utf8},
)
print(df.shape)      # (1200000, 5)
print(df.head(3))
# ┌────────────────┬──────────┬────────────┬───────────────┬───────────────┐
# │ meter_id       │ daily_kw │ date       │ region        │ customer_type │
# │ ---            │ ---      │ ---        │ ---           │ ---           │
# │ str            │ f64      │ str        │ str           │ str           │
# ╞════════════════╪══════════╪════════════╪═══════════════╪═══════════════╡
# │ IE-DUB-00142   │ 28.5     │ 2025-01-15 │ DUBLIN-NORTH  │ RESIDENTIAL   │
# │ IE-DUB-00143   │ 45.2     │ 2025-01-15 │ DUBLIN-NORTH  │ COMMERCIAL    │
# │ IE-CRK-00201   │ 12.8     │ 2025-01-15 │ CORK-EAST     │ RESIDENTIAL   │
# └────────────────┴──────────┴────────────┴───────────────┴───────────────┘

print(f'Memory: {df.estimated_size("mb"):.1f} MB')  # ~380 MB (vs 2,400 MB for list[dict])

Polars reads the CSV directly into a columnar DataFrame, parsing types on the fly. The dtypes parameter specifies column types (analogous to the explicit schema in Module 5 junior's pyarrow lesson). The estimated_size() method reports the DataFrame's memory footprint: approximately 380 MB for 1.2 million rows with 5 columns — versus 2,400 MB for the same data as a list[dict]. The 6x memory reduction comes from the columnar layout: column names are stored once (not per row), and values are stored in typed arrays (not boxed Python objects). Polars also reads CSV faster than Python's csv module because the parsing is implemented in Rust, not Python.
Basic Polars Operations

import polars as pl

# Filter: keep valid readings
valid = df.filter(
    (pl.col('daily_kw') >= 0) & (pl.col('daily_kw') <= 5000)
)

# Transform: add derived columns
silver = valid.with_columns([
    (pl.col('daily_kw') / 1000).alias('daily_mwh'),
    pl.col('region').str.to_uppercase().alias('region_normalized'),
    pl.lit('2025-01-15T03:00:00+00:00').alias('loaded_at'),
])

# Aggregate: regional summary (Gold layer)
gold = silver.group_by('region_normalized').agg([
    pl.col('daily_kw').sum().alias('total_kw'),
    pl.col('daily_kw').mean().alias('avg_kw'),
    pl.col('meter_id').n_unique().alias('meter_count'),
])
print(gold)

The Polars API uses expressions: pl.col('daily_kw') references a column, and methods chain operations on it (.sum(), .mean(), .str.to_uppercase()). The filter, with_columns, and group_by operations create new DataFrames without modifying the original — the same immutability pattern as the junior-level list[dict] approach, but with columnar memory efficiency and native parallel execution. At CelticWind, Polars replaces list[dict] for the Silver-to-Gold aggregation step: the 8-region group-by with sum, mean, and count completes in 0.3 seconds on 1.2M rows, versus 14 seconds with the junior-level defaultdict pattern.

blocks.title: Polars is the bridge between Python and Spark
blocks.content: If you plan to learn PySpark (the next track in the curriculum), Polars is the ideal preparation. Both use lazy evaluation (lesson 6), both operate on columnar DataFrames, both use expression APIs (col(), when/then, group_by/agg), and both are built on Apache Arrow. The mental model you build with Polars — thinking in column expressions instead of row loops — transfers directly to PySpark. The syntax differs, but the concepts are identical.

The junior-level track used list[dict] for all data processing: filtering with comprehensions, transforming with map functions, and aggregating with defaultdict loops. Polars provides an alternative that is faster and more memory-efficient — but not always simpler or appropriate. This lesson compares the two approaches head-to-head on CelticWind's real workloads: the Silver validation and transformation step and the Gold regional aggregation step. The comparison is measured, not theoretical: profiling both approaches on 1.2M rows reveals exactly where Polars wins and where list[dict] remains the better choice.
The Benchmark

The numbers are unambiguous: Polars is 11x faster and uses 9x less memory for the complete Silver pipeline on 1.2M rows. The filter operation is the most dramatic difference (52x) because Polars evaluates the filter as a vectorized operation over contiguous arrays, while list[dict] iterates Python objects one at a time. The aggregate operation is 28x faster because Polars uses hash-based group-by with native parallelism, while defaultdict accumulates values in a Python loop. The memory reduction comes primarily from the columnar representation: dictionary overhead (key strings per row, Python object headers) accounts for 80% of list[dict] memory usage.
When list[dict] Is Still the Right Choice

Polars is not universally superior. Three scenarios favor list[dict]. First, complex per-row logic: if the transformation requires calling external functions per row (geocoding an address, querying a database per meter), the row-level iteration of list[dict] is natural, while Polars requires a map function or a UDF that negates the vectorization benefit. Second, small datasets: for datasets under 10,000 rows (reference tables, configuration lookups), the overhead of creating a Polars DataFrame exceeds the processing time — a list of 200 dictionaries processes in microseconds either way. Third, compatibility: the pipeline's readers and writers (from Module 5 junior) produce and consume list[dict]; converting to Polars for one step and back adds complexity.

At CelticWind, the guideline is: use Polars for steps that process more than 100,000 rows with vectorizable operations (filter, transform, aggregate). Use list[dict] for steps that process fewer than 100,000 rows, require per-row external calls, or interact with systems that expect dictionaries (JSON APIs, Pydantic validators). The Silver validation step uses list[dict] with chunked processing (because Pydantic validation is per-row). The Silver transformation and Gold aggregation steps use Polars (because normalization and aggregation are vectorizable). The two approaches coexist in the same pipeline, connected by conversion functions.

blocks.title: Do not convert back and forth unnecessarily
blocks.content: Converting list[dict] to a Polars DataFrame (pl.from_dicts(readings)) and back (df.to_dicts()) has overhead: approximately 1 second per million rows in each direction. If the pipeline converts to Polars for a 0.3-second aggregation and converts back, the conversion overhead (2 seconds) exceeds the aggregation benefit. At CelticWind, the rule is: convert once at the boundary (after validation, before aggregation) and stay in Polars for all subsequent analytical steps. The write step accepts Polars DataFrames directly (df.write_parquet(path)), avoiding the conversion back to list[dict].

Lessons 4 and 5 used Polars' eager API: each operation (filter, with_columns, group_by) executes immediately and returns a new DataFrame. Polars also provides a lazy API that builds a query plan without executing it — and then optimizes the plan before execution. This is the same concept as PySpark's Catalyst optimizer and SQL's query planner: the system sees the entire query before running any of it, enabling optimizations that the eager API cannot perform (predicate pushdown into file reads, projection pushdown to skip unused columns, common subexpression elimination). For CelticWind's analytical pipeline, lazy evaluation reduces memory usage by 60% because the optimizer eliminates intermediate DataFrames.
Lazy Evaluation with scan_csv

import polars as pl

# Lazy: build a query plan without executing
lazy_plan = (
    pl.scan_csv('data/bronze/meters_20250115.csv')  # does not read the file yet
    .filter(pl.col('daily_kw').cast(pl.Float64) >= 0)
    .filter(pl.col('daily_kw').cast(pl.Float64) <= 5000)
    .with_columns([
        (pl.col('daily_kw').cast(pl.Float64) / 1000).alias('daily_mwh'),
        pl.col('region').str.to_uppercase().alias('region_normalized'),
    ])
    .group_by('region_normalized')
    .agg([
        pl.col('daily_kw').cast(pl.Float64).sum().alias('total_kw'),
        pl.col('daily_kw').cast(pl.Float64).mean().alias('avg_kw'),
        pl.col('meter_id').n_unique().alias('meter_count'),
    ])
)

# Inspect the optimized plan
print(lazy_plan.explain(optimized=True))

# Execute: Polars reads the file, applies all operations in one pass
result = lazy_plan.collect()  # returns a DataFrame
print(result)

The scan_csv function returns a LazyFrame — a query plan, not data. Each subsequent operation (.filter, .with_columns, .group_by) adds a step to the plan without executing it. The .collect() call triggers execution: Polars optimizes the plan (pushing filters into the CSV reader so filtered-out rows are never materialized, projecting only the columns that appear in the query) and executes it in a single pass over the file. The optimized plan reads fewer bytes from disk and allocates fewer intermediate DataFrames than the eager equivalent.
Optimization Benefits

Polars' lazy optimizer performs three key optimizations. Predicate pushdown: the filter conditions (daily_kw >= 0 and daily_kw <= 5000) are pushed into the CSV reader, so rows that fail the filter are never materialized as DataFrame rows. Projection pushdown: if the query uses only meter_id, daily_kw, and region, the other columns (date, customer_type) are never read from the file. Common subexpression elimination: the cast(pl.Float64) on daily_kw is computed once and reused across the filter, the with_columns, and the aggregation. At CelticWind, lazy evaluation reduced the Gold aggregation pipeline's peak memory from 475 MB (eager) to 180 MB (lazy) because intermediate DataFrames were eliminated.

blocks.title: Lazy evaluation is the mental model for distributed data engineering
blocks.content: PySpark, Spark SQL, and dbt all use lazy evaluation: you describe the transformations, and the engine optimizes and executes them. Learning Polars' lazy API now builds the intuition you need for these frameworks. The key habit: describe what you want (filter, transform, aggregate) without thinking about execution order. Let the optimizer decide the most efficient execution strategy. This is a shift from imperative thinking ('read the file, then filter, then transform') to declarative thinking ('I want filtered, transformed, aggregated results from this file').

CelticWind's SCADA turbine telemetry arrives as Parquet files from the SCADA system. The junior-level pipeline reads Parquet with pyarrow, converts to list[dict] for processing, and writes the result as Parquet with pyarrow — converting between columnar (Arrow) and row-oriented (dict) representations twice. Each conversion copies the data: Arrow columns are copied into Python dictionaries (read path), and dictionaries are copied into Arrow columns (write path). For 4,800 turbines with hourly readings (172,800 rows per file), the conversion overhead is small. For the historical reprocessing job (365 files × 172,800 rows = 63 million rows), the conversion overhead is significant. Zero-copy transfer eliminates this overhead by keeping the data in Arrow format throughout the pipeline.
Arrow as the Interchange Format

import pyarrow as pa
import pyarrow.parquet as pq
import polars as pl

# Read Parquet into pyarrow Table (columnar, zero-copy ready)
table = pq.read_table(
    'data/bronze/scada_20250115.parquet',
    columns=['turbine_id', 'power_output', 'ts'],  # projection pushdown
)
print(f'pyarrow: {table.num_rows} rows, {table.nbytes / 1024 / 1024:.1f} MB')

# Convert to Polars DataFrame — ZERO COPY (shares Arrow buffers)
df = pl.from_arrow(table)
print(f'Polars: {df.shape[0]} rows, {df.estimated_size("mb"):.1f} MB')  # same memory

# Process with Polars (filter, transform, aggregate)
result = df.filter(pl.col('power_output') > 0).group_by('turbine_id').agg(
    pl.col('power_output').mean().alias('avg_output'),
)

# Convert back to pyarrow Table — ZERO COPY
output_table = result.to_arrow()

# Write to Parquet
pq.write_table(output_table, 'data/silver/scada_summary.parquet', compression='snappy')

The pl.from_arrow(table) call converts a pyarrow Table to a Polars DataFrame without copying the data. Both the pyarrow Table and the Polars DataFrame point to the same Arrow memory buffers. The result.to_arrow() call converts back to pyarrow for writing — again without copying. The entire read-process-write cycle uses one set of Arrow buffers: no list[dict] conversion, no dictionary construction, no memory duplication. At CelticWind, the SCADA processing pipeline uses this zero-copy path exclusively: pyarrow reads the Parquet, Polars processes it, and pyarrow writes the result — with the data living in Arrow format throughout.
Column Pruning at the Source

The pq.read_table() call with columns=['turbine_id', 'power_output', 'ts'] reads only 3 of the SCADA file's 12 columns. Because Parquet stores data in a columnar format, the 9 unused columns are never read from disk — not read and then filtered, but physically skipped during the file scan. For CelticWind's SCADA files (12 columns, 172,800 rows per file), reading 3 columns instead of 12 reduces the disk read from 45 MB to 12 MB and the in-memory footprint from 95 MB to 25 MB. This projection pushdown is one of Parquet's key advantages over CSV, where every byte of every row must be read regardless of which columns the pipeline needs.

blocks.title: Keep data in Arrow format between steps
blocks.content: The most common performance mistake with pyarrow is converting to list[dict] for processing and converting back to pyarrow for writing. Each conversion copies the entire dataset. If the processing can be done with Polars (which operates on Arrow buffers natively) or with pyarrow's compute module (which provides filter, cast, and aggregate functions on Arrow tables), the data stays in Arrow format throughout the pipeline — zero copies, minimal memory overhead. At CelticWind, the rule is: convert to list[dict] only when per-row logic is required (Pydantic validation, external API lookups); use Arrow-native processing for everything else.

Lesson 2 taught tracemalloc for development-time memory measurement. Production pipelines need continuous memory monitoring that goes beyond development profiling: tracking peak memory per run over time to detect gradual growth, identifying memory leaks where objects accumulate across chunks or across pipeline runs, and alerting when memory usage approaches the server's physical limit before the pipeline crashes. This lesson covers integrating memory measurement into the pipeline's observability infrastructure — the structured logging and metrics that Module 9 will build into a complete monitoring stack.
Logging Peak Memory per Pipeline Run

import tracemalloc
import resource
import logging
from contextlib import contextmanager

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@contextmanager
def memory_tracking(pipeline_name: str):
    """Context manager that tracks and logs memory usage."""
    tracemalloc.start()
    try:
        yield
    finally:
        current, peak = tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()
        tracemalloc.stop()

        # Also get RSS (Resident Set Size) from the OS
        rss_mb = resource.getrusage(resource.RUSAGE_SELF).ru_maxrss / 1024  # Linux: KB

        logger.info(
            'Memory summary: pipeline=%s peak_traced_mb=%.1f rss_mb=%.1f',
            pipeline_name,
            peak / (1024 * 1024),
            rss_mb,
        )

# Usage
with memory_tracking('silver_meter_readings'):
    run_chunked_pipeline(config)
# Log: Memory summary: pipeline=silver_meter_readings peak_traced_mb=185.3 rss_mb=220.4

The context manager tracks two memory metrics: tracemalloc's peak (Python-level allocations only) and the OS-level RSS (total process memory including the Python runtime, loaded libraries, and memory-mapped files). The difference between the two reveals non-Python memory usage: pyarrow's Arrow buffers, Polars' Rust-allocated memory, and the Python runtime overhead are included in RSS but not in tracemalloc. At CelticWind, both metrics are logged as structured key-value pairs (peak_traced_mb, rss_mb) so the monitoring dashboard can track them over time and alert when peak memory approaches 80% of the server's capacity.
Detecting Memory Leaks

A memory leak in a data pipeline typically manifests as growing memory across chunks or across runs: each chunk processes 50,000 rows, but peak memory grows from 185 MB (chunk 1) to 190 MB (chunk 2) to 195 MB (chunk 3) and eventually exhausts memory. The usual cause: a reference to the chunk's data persists after the chunk is processed — appended to a list for later analysis, stored in a module-level variable for debugging, or held by a closure in a callback function. At CelticWind, memory leak detection is a scheduled health check: the pipeline logs peak_traced_mb per chunk, and if the value increases by more than 5% across 10 consecutive chunks, a warning is logged and the on-call engineer investigates.

blocks.title: tracemalloc adds 10-30% overhead
blocks.content: tracemalloc tracks every Python allocation, which adds CPU and memory overhead. In production, this overhead can push a pipeline that runs at 95% capacity over the edge. At CelticWind, tracemalloc is enabled by default in the development and staging environments (where the overhead is acceptable) and disabled in production (where every megabyte matters). Production memory monitoring uses resource.getrusage() instead — which reports OS-level memory with zero overhead because the OS tracks it regardless. Detailed tracemalloc analysis is reserved for incident investigation: a developer enables it temporarily when a memory issue is suspected.

Memory optimization adds code complexity: chunked processing requires managing chunk boundaries and aggregation merges, Polars requires learning a new API and converting between representations, and zero-copy Arrow paths require careful attention to which operations copy data and which do not. This complexity has a maintenance cost — every future developer who modifies the pipeline must understand the chunking logic, the Polars expressions, and the Arrow buffer management. The question is not 'can we reduce memory usage?' but 'does the memory reduction justify the complexity?' At CelticWind, the answer is 'yes' for the 1.2M-row Silver pipeline (which would crash without optimization) and 'no' for the 200-row substation reference pipeline (which uses 50 KB regardless of optimization approach).
The Decision Framework

rows: No optimization needed — the pipeline has 2x headroom for data growth and intermediate copies
Add del for large intermediates and monitor memory growth trend. Consider chunking if growth exceeds 10% per quarter
Optimize: chunked processing for I/O, Polars for analytical transforms, pyarrow zero-copy for Parquet pipelines
No optimization needed regardless of per-row memory — total memory is negligible
Memory budget is shared — optimize if the pipeline's peak exceeds 30% of available RAM (leaving room for cohabitants)

blocks.content: The framework uses the ratio of peak memory to available RAM as the decision metric — not the absolute memory usage. A pipeline that uses 2 GB on a 16 GB server (12.5%) is fine. The same pipeline on a 4 GB server (50%) needs monitoring. On a 2 GB container (100%) it crashes. At CelticWind, the production server has 4 GB, and the pipeline's memory budget is 2 GB (leaving 2 GB for the OS, Python runtime, and logging). The chunked pipeline's peak of 185 MB fits comfortably within this budget, with 10x headroom for data growth. The Polars-based Gold aggregation uses 180 MB peak — also well within budget.
The Complexity Cost of Each Approach

Explicit del (simplest): adds 1-2 lines per intermediate variable. Minimal complexity, minimal benefit (prevents peak-stacking but does not reduce per-step memory). Chunked processing (moderate): requires chunk-size tuning, append-mode writes, and aggregation merge logic. The chunked read function adds 15 lines; the pipeline loop adds 10 lines. Polars (moderate-high): requires learning the Polars API, managing conversion boundaries between list[dict] and Polars DataFrames, and potentially maintaining two code paths (Polars for large datasets, list[dict] for small datasets). Zero-copy Arrow (highest): requires understanding Arrow buffer ownership, avoiding operations that copy data, and debugging memory issues that are invisible to tracemalloc (Arrow allocations are not tracked). At CelticWind, the complexity budget is: del everywhere, chunking for the Silver pipeline, Polars for Gold aggregation, Arrow zero-copy for SCADA Parquet processing.

blocks.title: Memory optimization follows the 80/20 rule
blocks.content: For most pipelines, 80% of the memory is consumed by 20% of the code — typically the initial data load and one or two large intermediate results. Optimizing those 2-3 lines (adding del, switching to chunked reads, using Polars for the aggregation) captures most of the benefit. Optimizing the remaining 80% of the code (small variables, temporary strings, function call overhead) provides negligible memory reduction for significant complexity. At CelticWind, the initial tracemalloc analysis identified 3 allocation sites that accounted for 92% of peak memory. Optimizing those 3 sites reduced peak memory from 2.4 GB to 185 MB.

This module taught the three approaches to memory-efficient data processing: chunked processing for constant-memory I/O (reading and writing data in batches), Polars for memory-efficient analytical transforms (columnar representation with 6x less memory and 11x faster execution), and pyarrow zero-copy for Parquet-based pipelines (keeping data in Arrow format throughout the read-process-write cycle). It also taught the measurement discipline: tracemalloc for development profiling, resource.getrusage for production monitoring, and the decision framework that determines when optimization is justified. This lesson assembles the patterns into CelticWind's production memory-efficient pipeline.
The Memory Architecture

blocks.title: CelticWind's memory-efficient pipeline architecture
blocks.content:
BRONZE (CSV)                   SILVER (validation)              SILVER (transform)               GOLD
┌──────────────────┐     ┌────────────────────┐    ┌────────────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐
│ Chunked CSV read │───▶│ Pydantic validation│───▶│ Polars DataFrame   │───▶│ Polars lazy   │
│ 50K rows/chunk   │     │ list[dict] chunks  │    │ (from validated   │    │ group_by +    │
│                  │     │ ~100 MB peak       │    │  list[dict])       │    │ agg           │
│ Memory: ~100 MB  │     │                    │    │ ~380 MB           │    │ ~180 MB       │
└──────────────────┘     └────────────────────┘    └────────────────────┘    └───────────────┘

SCADA (Parquet)                 SCADA processing
┌──────────────────┐     ┌────────────────────┐
│ pyarrow read     │───▶│ Polars (zero-copy) │───▶ pyarrow write
│ column pruning   │     │ filter + aggregate │    (zero-copy)
│ ~25 MB           │     │ ~30 MB             │
└──────────────────┘     └────────────────────┘

Peak memory budget: 2 GB on a 4 GB server
Actual peak: ~380 MB (Polars Silver transform step)
blocks.caption: The pipeline uses three memory strategies. The CSV meter readings path uses chunked list[dict] for validation (Pydantic requires per-row processing) and Polars for transformation and aggregation. The SCADA Parquet path uses pyarrow with zero-copy Polars conversion throughout. Both paths stay within the 2 GB memory budget on the 4 GB production server.

blocks.content: The architecture uses the right tool for each step. Chunked list[dict] for the Bronze CSV read and Pydantic validation (per-row processing that Polars cannot vectorize). Polars DataFrames for the Silver transformation and Gold aggregation (column-level operations that benefit from vectorization and columnar memory). pyarrow with zero-copy Polars for the SCADA Parquet pipeline (data stays in Arrow format from read to write). The peak memory across all steps is approximately 380 MB (the Polars Silver transformation of 1.2M rows) — well within the 2 GB budget, with 5x headroom for data growth.

The modern patterns phase (Modules 1–3) is complete. Module 1 taught the architecture (protocols, DI, composition) that makes the pipeline extensible and testable. Module 2 taught concurrency (threading, multiprocessing, asyncio) that makes the pipeline fast. Module 3 taught memory efficiency (chunking, Polars, Arrow) that makes the pipeline scalable. Together, these three modules transform the junior-level pipeline from a single-threaded, memory-hungry script into a concurrent, memory-efficient, architecturally sound production system. The next phase (Modules 4–5) builds the framework and configuration infrastructure that makes this production system manageable by a 15-person team across 12 pipelines.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 4 teaches pipeline framework design: the registry pattern, plugin discovery, pipeline DAGs, and YAML-driven configuration that let CelticWind's 3 teams define 12 pipelines using shared infrastructure. The memory-efficient components from this module (chunked readers, Polars transformers, Arrow writers) become pluggable components in the framework — registered by name and instantiated from configuration. The architecture from Module 1 (protocols, DI) provides the interface contracts; the framework from Module 4 provides the wiring.

modules.title: Pipeline Framework Design
modules.description: Design pipeline frameworks with plugin architectures, registry patterns, and pipeline DAGs — the patterns that let other engineers extend the pipeline without modifying its core.

blocks.content: CelticWind now operates 12 pipelines across 3 teams. Each pipeline follows the same structure: read data from a source, validate it, transform it, write the output, and produce a validation report. The differences between pipelines are their components: the billing pipeline reads CSV and validates strictly, the analytics pipeline reads Parquet and validates leniently, the operations pipeline reads from an API and validates with custom rules. Module 1 taught protocols and dependency injection so that these components are swappable. But 12 pipeline scripts still means 12 files that each instantiate a SilverPipeline with different constructor arguments — 12 copies of the wiring logic that connects readers to validators to writers.
