The Duplication Problem at Scale

# billing/pipeline.py — one of 12 pipeline scripts
from celticwind.pipeline import SilverPipeline
from celticwind.readers import CsvReader
from celticwind.validators import BatchValidator, StrictValidator
from celticwind.transformers import RegionNormalizer, DerivedFieldAdder
from celticwind.writers import ParquetWriter

def main(config: dict):
    pipeline = SilverPipeline(
        reader=CsvReader(encoding='utf-8', delimiter=','),
        validator=BatchValidator(StrictValidator(max_kw=5000)),
        transformer=ChainedTransformer([RegionNormalizer(), DerivedFieldAdder()]),
        writer=ParquetWriter(compression='snappy'),
    )
    pipeline.run(config['input_path'], config['output_path'])

# analytics/pipeline.py — nearly identical, different components
# operations/pipeline.py — nearly identical, different components
# ... 9 more files with the same structure

Each script imports the same pipeline class, instantiates the same pattern of reader + validator + transformer + writer, and calls the same run method. The only variation is which concrete classes are instantiated and what constructor arguments they receive. This is a configuration problem masquerading as a code problem: the pipeline structure is fixed, and the variation is in the component selection — which should be expressed as data (configuration), not as code (12 Python scripts). A framework eliminates the duplication by defining the pipeline structure once and letting each pipeline declare its specific components in a configuration file.
What a Framework Provides

rows: Copy an existing script, modify the imports and constructor args
Write a YAML file specifying the component names and parameters
Modify 12 files, hope they are all consistent
Modify the framework once — all 12 pipelines inherit the change
Test each script separately, duplicating test setup
Test the framework's wiring logic once; test individual components separately
Read 12 scripts to understand the patterns and variations
Read 1 framework + the pipeline YAML to understand any specific pipeline

blocks.title: A framework is justified by duplication, not by ambition
blocks.content: CelticWind built its framework when the 8th pipeline was added and the team was spending more time maintaining boilerplate than writing business logic. A team with 3 pipelines does not need a framework — 3 well-structured scripts with shared utility modules are simpler, more readable, and easier to debug. The threshold at CelticWind: when more than half the code in a new pipeline is copy-pasted from an existing one, the shared structure should become a framework. Below that threshold, the abstraction overhead exceeds the duplication cost.

The framework needs to map component names (strings from a YAML configuration file) to component classes (Python classes that implement the appropriate protocol). The registry pattern provides this mapping: a central dictionary that associates string names with factory functions or classes. The pipeline configuration says reader: csv, and the registry returns the CsvReader class. The pipeline configuration says validator: strict, and the registry returns the StrictValidator class. The registry decouples the configuration (which uses human-readable string names) from the implementation (which uses Python classes).
A Type-Safe Registry

The Registry class stores a dictionary mapping string keys to factory functions. The register method adds entries; the create method looks up a key, calls the factory with the configuration dictionary, and returns the instance. The error message for unknown keys lists all available entries — so a typo in the YAML file (reader: cvs instead of reader: csv) produces a helpful message rather than a cryptic KeyError. At CelticWind, four registries exist (readers, validators, transformers, writers), and each is populated during application startup before any pipeline configuration is loaded.
Registering Components

Each factory function takes a configuration dictionary and returns a fully configured component instance. The factory handles the mapping from configuration keys (encoding, delimiter, max_kw) to constructor arguments. Lambda factories work for simple cases; named functions are preferred when the construction logic is more complex (error handling, default value computation). At CelticWind, the registration calls live in a setup_registries() function called during application startup. Each team can register their own custom components without modifying the framework — the operations team registers api for their ApiReader, and the analytics team registers partitioned_parquet for their PartitionedParquetReader.

blocks.title: The registry replaces if/elif chains with a dictionary lookup
blocks.content: Without the registry, the framework would use if reader_type == 'csv': return CsvReader(...) elif reader_type == 'parquet': return ParquetReader(...) — a chain that grows with every new reader type and requires modifying the framework code. The registry replaces this chain with a dictionary lookup that external code can extend. This is the Open/Closed Principle in practice: the framework is open for extension (register new components) and closed for modification (no framework code changes when a new component is added).

Lesson 2's registry requires explicit registration calls: reader_registry.register('csv', create_csv_reader). This works when all components live in the same package. When the operations team develops their ApiReader in a separate package (celticwind-ops), they must import it in the main application's startup code — creating a dependency from the framework to the team's package. Entry points (defined in pyproject.toml) solve this: a package declares its contributions to a registry, and the framework discovers them automatically at startup. The operations team registers their ApiReader in their own pyproject.toml, and the framework finds it without any import or registration call in the main application.
Declaring Entry Points

# celticwind-ops/pyproject.toml — the operations team's package
[project]
name = "celticwind-ops"
version = "1.2.0"

[project.entry-points."celticwind.readers"]
api = "celticwind_ops.readers:create_api_reader"

[project.entry-points."celticwind.validators"]
ops_custom = "celticwind_ops.validators:create_ops_validator"

# celticwind-analytics/pyproject.toml — the analytics team's package
[project]
name = "celticwind-analytics"
version = "2.0.0"

[project.entry-points."celticwind.readers"]
partitioned_parquet = "celticwind_analytics.readers:create_partitioned_reader"

Each entry point declaration maps a name (api, ops_custom, partitioned_parquet) to a factory function in the team's package. The group name (celticwind.readers, celticwind.validators) identifies which registry the entry point belongs to. When the operations team installs their package (pip install celticwind-ops), the entry points are registered in the Python environment's metadata — discoverable through Python's importlib.metadata module. No import statement in the main application is needed, and no modification to the framework code is required. The team's package is self-registering.
Auto-Discovery at Startup

from importlib.metadata import entry_points
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def auto_register(registry: Registry, group: str) -> None:
    """Discover and register all entry points for a registry group."""
    discovered = entry_points(group=group)
    for ep in discovered:
        try:
            factory = ep.load()  # imports the module and returns the function
            registry.register(ep.name, factory)
            logger.info('Auto-registered %s from %s', ep.name, ep.dist.name)
        except Exception as e:
            logger.error('Failed to load entry point %s: %s', ep.name, e)

# At framework startup: discover all plugins
auto_register(reader_registry, 'celticwind.readers')
auto_register(validator_registry, 'celticwind.validators')
auto_register(transformer_registry, 'celticwind.transformers')
auto_register(writer_registry, 'celticwind.writers')

# Now reader_registry contains: csv, parquet, api, partitioned_parquet
# — without the framework importing any team's package directly

The auto_register function iterates over all entry points in a group, loads each factory function (which triggers the import of the team's module), and registers it in the registry. If a team's package is not installed in the current environment, its entry points are simply absent — no error, no missing import. The framework works with whatever plugins are available. At CelticWind, the production environment has all 3 team packages installed; the billing team's development environment has only the core package and celticwind-billing. This means the billing team can develop and test without installing the operations or analytics packages.

blocks.title: Entry points are how pip, pytest, and Flask discover plugins
blocks.content: The entry point mechanism is not a CelticWind invention — it is the standard Python plugin discovery pattern. pytest discovers test plugins via the pytest11 entry point group. Flask discovers extensions via flask.commands. pip discovers build backends via build-system.requires. By using the same mechanism, CelticWind's framework follows the ecosystem convention that Python engineers already understand. A new team member who has written a pytest plugin or a Flask extension will recognize the pattern immediately.

CelticWind's Silver pipeline executes a linear sequence: read → validate → transform → write. The enrichment pipeline is more complex: read meters, read weather (independent of meters), read prices (independent of meters and weather), enrich meters with weather data, enrich the result with price data, then write the enriched output. The weather and price reads are independent and can execute concurrently (Module 2), but the enrichment steps depend on all three reads completing first. This dependency structure is a directed acyclic graph (DAG): each step declares which other steps it depends on, and the framework resolves the execution order.
Defining Steps and Dependencies

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class StepDefinition:
    """Defines a single pipeline step and its dependencies."""
    name: str
    component_type: str  # registry key: 'reader', 'validator', 'transformer', 'writer'
    component_name: str  # registry entry: 'csv', 'strict', 'region_normalizer'
    config: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    depends_on: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

# CelticWind's enrichment pipeline as a DAG
enrichment_dag = [
    StepDefinition('read_meters', 'reader', 'csv', {'path': 'meters.csv'}, depends_on=[]),
    StepDefinition('read_weather', 'reader', 'api', {'source': 'met_eireann'}, depends_on=[]),
    StepDefinition('read_prices', 'reader', 'api', {'source': 'epex'}, depends_on=[]),
    StepDefinition('validate', 'validator', 'strict', {'max_kw': 5000}, depends_on=['read_meters']),
    StepDefinition('enrich_weather', 'transformer', 'weather_join',
                   {}, depends_on=['validate', 'read_weather']),
    StepDefinition('enrich_prices', 'transformer', 'price_join',
                   {}, depends_on=['enrich_weather', 'read_prices']),
    StepDefinition('write_silver', 'writer', 'parquet',
                   {'path': 'silver/enriched'}, depends_on=['enrich_prices']),
]

Each StepDefinition declares its name, the registry it draws from (component_type), the specific component (component_name), configuration parameters, and the list of steps it depends on. The read_meters, read_weather, and read_prices steps have no dependencies — they can execute concurrently. The validate step depends on read_meters (it needs the meter data). The enrich_weather step depends on both validate and read_weather (it needs validated meters and weather forecasts). The framework resolves this DAG to determine the execution order: the three reads run first (concurrently), then validation, then the two enrichment steps in sequence, then the write.
Topological Sort for Execution Order

The topological sort groups steps into layers: all steps in a layer have their dependencies satisfied by previous layers and can execute concurrently (using ThreadPoolExecutor from Module 2). The circular dependency check prevents infinite loops: if step A depends on B and B depends on A, the algorithm detects the cycle and raises a clear error. At CelticWind, the framework resolves the DAG at pipeline construction time (before any data is processed), catching configuration errors like circular dependencies and missing step references early rather than mid-execution.

blocks.title: This is the same model that Airflow uses
blocks.content: Apache Airflow defines workflows as DAGs of tasks with dependencies. The framework in this lesson uses the same model at a smaller scale: steps are tasks, depends_on declares dependencies, and the topological sort determines execution order. If you continue to the Airflow track, you will recognize the DAG concept immediately. The key difference: Airflow manages scheduling across time (run this DAG daily at 03:00), while CelticWind's framework manages execution within a single run (these steps run in this order).

Every pipeline step needs logging (log the start and end), timing (record how long the step took), error handling (catch exceptions and decide whether to retry, skip, or fail), and metrics (count rows processed, rows rejected). Without a framework, each step implements these cross-cutting concerns individually — and inconsistently. The framework provides lifecycle hooks: before_step (logging, validation), execute (the actual processing), after_step (metrics, cleanup), and on_error (error handling, quarantine). The hooks apply the cross-cutting concerns uniformly to every step without requiring each step to implement them.
The Step Lifecycle

The StepRunner wraps any component's process method with the lifecycle: before hooks run first (logging the start, validating input schema), the component executes, and after hooks run last (recording metrics, logging the result). If the component raises an exception, on_error hooks run instead (logging the error, recording failure metrics). The hooks are injected as a list — the framework can apply different hook sets to different steps. At CelticWind, every step gets the LoggingHook and the MetricsHook; steps that process external data additionally get the RetryHook.
Common Hooks

The StepHook protocol defines the three lifecycle methods. LoggingHook provides structured logging for every step without the step itself containing any logging code. MetricsHook collects timing and success/failure data for the monitoring dashboard (Module 9). Additional hooks can be added without modifying the StepRunner or the steps: a RetryHook that retries failed steps, a SchemaValidationHook that checks input/output schemas, or a NotificationHook that sends a Slack message on failure. At CelticWind, hooks are configured per pipeline in the YAML file: hooks: [logging, metrics, retry] for API-sourced pipelines, hooks: [logging, metrics] for file-sourced pipelines.

blocks.title: Hooks separate infrastructure from business logic
blocks.content: The RegionNormalizer transformer knows how to normalize region codes. It does not know how to log its start time, record its duration, or retry on failure — that is infrastructure, not business logic. Hooks keep the separation clean: the transformer implements only its transformation, and the hooks wrap it with infrastructure behavior. This means a change to the logging format (adding a correlation ID from Module 9) requires modifying one hook, not 12 pipeline steps.

Lessons 2-5 built the framework's components: registries for component lookup, DAGs for dependency resolution, and lifecycle hooks for cross-cutting concerns. This lesson connects them with YAML-driven pipeline definitions. Instead of writing Python code to instantiate and wire pipeline components, the engineer writes a YAML file that declares the pipeline's steps, their component types, and their parameters. The framework reads the YAML, resolves the components from the registries, resolves the execution order from the DAG, and runs the pipeline. A new pipeline is created by writing a YAML file — no Python code required.
The Pipeline YAML Format

# pipelines/silver_billing.yml
pipeline:
  name: silver_billing
  description: "Bronze-to-Silver pipeline for billing meter readings"
  hooks: [logging, metrics]

steps:
  - name: read_meters
    type: reader
    component: csv
    config:
      encoding: utf-8
      delimiter: ","

  - name: validate
    type: validator
    component: strict
    config:
      max_kw: 5000
    depends_on: [read_meters]

  - name: transform
    type: transformer
    component: chain
    config:
      steps: [region_normalizer, derived_fields, timestamp_utc]
    depends_on: [validate]

  - name: write_silver
    type: writer
    component: parquet
    config:
      compression: snappy
      partition_by: [date]
    depends_on: [transform]

The YAML file declares the pipeline's name, its lifecycle hooks, and its steps. Each step specifies the registry to draw from (type: reader), the component name within that registry (component: csv), the configuration dictionary passed to the factory function, and the dependencies (depends_on: [read_meters]). The framework reads this YAML, creates each component from the registries using reader_registry.create('csv', config), builds the DAG from the depends_on declarations, and attaches the specified hooks. The result is a fully configured, ready-to-run pipeline constructed entirely from the YAML specification.
The Pipeline Builder

import yaml
from pathlib import Path

def build_pipeline_from_yaml(yaml_path: Path) -> 'Pipeline':
    """Build a pipeline from a YAML definition file."""
    with open(yaml_path) as f:
        spec = yaml.safe_load(f)

    pipeline_config = spec['pipeline']
    step_defs = []

    for step_spec in spec['steps']:
        registry = get_registry(step_spec['type'])  # reader_registry, etc.
        component = registry.create(step_spec['component'], step_spec.get('config', {}))
        step_defs.append(StepDefinition(
            name=step_spec['name'],
            component=component,
            depends_on=step_spec.get('depends_on', []),
        ))

    hooks = [hook_registry.create(h, {}) for h in pipeline_config.get('hooks', [])]
    execution_order = resolve_execution_order(step_defs)

    return Pipeline(
        name=pipeline_config['name'],
        steps=step_defs,
        execution_order=execution_order,
        hooks=hooks,
    )

The build_pipeline_from_yaml function is the framework's entry point: it reads the YAML, creates components from registries, resolves the DAG, and returns a ready-to-run Pipeline object. Errors in the YAML (unknown component names, circular dependencies, missing configuration keys) are caught at build time — before any data is processed. At CelticWind, each team maintains their own pipeline YAML files in their repository, and the framework builds and runs them during the scheduled pipeline execution. Adding a new pipeline is a pull request with a single YAML file and no Python code changes.

blocks.title: YAML is for component selection, not for logic
blocks.content: The YAML file declares which components to use and how to configure them. It does not contain conditional logic (if region == DUBLIN then ...), loop constructs, or inline Python code. When the pipeline requires conditional behavior (different validation rules for different regions), the condition is implemented in a component (a RoutingValidator that delegates to region-specific validators based on data content), not in the YAML. Putting logic in YAML creates a meta-programming problem where the configuration file becomes a programming language without a debugger, type checker, or test framework.

The enrichment pipeline's weather step produces output with columns [meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, temp_c, wind_speed]. The price enrichment step expects input with columns [meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, temp_c, wind_speed] — the exact output of the weather step. If a developer modifies the weather step to rename temp_c to temperature_celsius, the price step fails at runtime with a KeyError. Data contracts prevent this: each step declares the schema of its output, and the framework validates that connected steps have compatible schemas before any data is processed.
Defining Schema Contracts

Each SchemaContract declares the columns a step produces (required_columns) and the columns it might produce depending on data availability (optional_columns). The satisfies method checks whether a producer's output meets a consumer's input requirements by comparing the column sets. The weather enrichment step's output contract includes temp_c and wind_speed; the price enrichment step's input contract requires them. If the weather step's contract changes (temp_c removed), the framework detects the incompatibility at construction time — before the pipeline runs — and reports the specific missing columns.
Contract Validation at Build Time

The framework validates contracts during build_pipeline_from_yaml: for each step that depends on another step, the producer's output contract must satisfy the consumer's input contract. If validation fails, the framework raises a ContractViolationError with the step names, the missing columns, and a suggestion for which contract needs updating. At CelticWind, contract violations are caught in CI: the pipeline YAML files are built (but not executed) during the CI test phase, and contract mismatches fail the build before the code reaches staging. This turns schema compatibility from a runtime error (discovered at 03:00 AM when the pipeline crashes) into a build-time error (discovered during the pull request review).

blocks.title: Contracts are documentation that the framework enforces
blocks.content: Without contracts, the connection between steps is implicit: the developer must read the weather step's code to know what columns it produces, and the price step's code to know what columns it expects. With contracts, the connection is explicit: the contract declarations document the interface, and the framework enforces the documentation at build time. When a developer modifies a step's output, they must update the contract — and the framework verifies that all downstream consumers are still satisfied.

Module 7 junior taught error handling for individual functions (try/except, retry, quarantine). Framework-level error recovery operates at a higher abstraction: when a pipeline step fails, the framework decides what happens next. Should it retry the step (transient API error)? Skip the step and continue with partial data (optional weather enrichment failed)? Fail the entire pipeline immediately (required meter read failed)? The recovery strategy is configured per step in the pipeline YAML, and the framework applies it automatically through the lifecycle hooks.
The Four Recovery Strategies

rows: Stop the pipeline immediately. No downstream steps execute.
The step is critical — the pipeline cannot produce valid output without it
read_meters: the pipeline has nothing to process without meter data
Retry the step up to N times with backoff. Fail after exhausting retries.
The step calls an external system that may have transient failures
read_weather: API may return 503 during maintenance windows
Log the failure and continue without this step's output. Downstream steps receive partial data.
The step is optional enrichment — the pipeline can produce useful output without it
enrich_prices: billing can proceed with meters alone if prices are unavailable
Isolate failing records, continue processing valid records
The step validates or transforms individual records, and partial output is acceptable
validate: reject bad rows but process the remaining 99.97%

blocks.content: Configuring Recovery in YAML

# pipelines/enrichment.yml (excerpt)
steps:
  - name: read_meters
    type: reader
    component: csv
    recovery: fail_fast  # pipeline stops if meters are unavailable
    depends_on: []

  - name: read_weather
    type: reader
    component: api
    config: {source: met_eireann}
    recovery: retry
    retry_config: {max_retries: 3, backoff_factor: 2.0}
    depends_on: []

  - name: enrich_prices
    type: transformer
    component: price_join
    recovery: skip  # pipeline continues without prices if API is down
    depends_on: [validate, read_prices]

Each step declares its recovery strategy and any strategy-specific configuration (retry count, backoff factor). The framework's StepRunner reads the recovery strategy and wraps the step execution accordingly: fail_fast re-raises the exception immediately, retry wraps the step in the retry_with_backoff function from Module 7 junior, skip catches the exception and logs it but lets the pipeline continue, and quarantine delegates to the validation function's per-record error handling. At CelticWind, the recovery strategy is reviewed during the pipeline design phase: the billing team requires fail_fast for every critical step, while the analytics team uses skip liberally because partial data is preferable to no data for their exploratory analyses.

blocks.title: Skip is the most dangerous strategy
blocks.content: The skip strategy lets the pipeline continue without a step's output. This is safe when the step is truly optional (weather enrichment for billing). It is dangerous when the step's absence silently corrupts downstream results (skipping validation means unvalidated data reaches Gold). At CelticWind, the skip strategy requires a review comment in the YAML explaining why the step is optional and what the downstream impact of skipping is. The monitoring dashboard flags every pipeline run where a step was skipped, and the weekly data quality review checks whether skipped steps are masking systematic failures.

sections.title: When NOT to Build a Framework

blocks.content: This module has taught a substantial framework: registries, entry points, DAGs, lifecycle hooks, YAML-driven configuration, data contracts, and error recovery strategies. Building this framework took CelticWind's platform team 6 weeks of engineering time. The framework serves 12 pipelines across 3 teams, and the time saved by eliminating boilerplate across those 12 pipelines exceeded the 6-week investment within 4 months. But a team with 3 pipelines would not break even for 18 months — and by then, the team's needs may have changed enough that the framework needs redesigning. The framework is an investment, and investments must be evaluated against their returns.
The Decision Thresholds

rows: No framework. Use shared utility modules and well-structured scripts.
The overhead of designing, building, and maintaining a framework exceeds the cost of duplicating 3 pipeline scripts.
Partial framework: shared registries and a pipeline factory function. No DAG, no hooks, no YAML.
The duplication is real but not severe enough to justify the full framework. A factory that instantiates pipelines from configuration captures 80% of the benefit.
Full framework: registries, DAG, hooks, YAML configuration, data contracts.
The duplication is severe, onboarding is slowed by inconsistency, and shared behavior changes require modifying multiple files.

blocks.content: The thresholds are guidelines, not rules. A team of 2 engineers with 5 nearly identical pipelines may benefit from a partial framework earlier than a team of 10 engineers with 5 very different pipelines. The key signal is the copy-paste ratio: when more than 50% of a new pipeline's code is copied from an existing pipeline, the shared structure should become a framework. Below that threshold, the pipelines are different enough that a framework would need so many extension points and special cases that it provides little value over individual scripts.
The Alternatives to a Custom Framework

Before building a custom framework, evaluate whether an existing tool provides the same capability. Apache Airflow provides DAG definition, scheduling, retry logic, and a UI for monitoring — covering most of what CelticWind's framework provides, plus scheduling that the framework does not. Luigi and Prefect provide similar capabilities with different APIs. The CelticWind framework exists because the team needed the pipeline infrastructure before the Airflow track in the curriculum — and because the framework teaches the patterns that Airflow uses internally. In a real organization, evaluate Airflow first; build a custom framework only if Airflow's overhead (its own infrastructure, its scheduling model, its learning curve) is not justified by the team's scale.

blocks.title: The best framework is the one you do not build
blocks.content: A shared utility module (common readers, common validators, a pipeline base class) provides 80% of a framework's deduplication benefit with 20% of the engineering cost. At CelticWind, the partial framework (registries + factory function) served the team for 6 months before the full framework was needed. The progression was: shared utilities (month 1-3) → partial framework with registries (month 4-9) → full framework with DAG and hooks (month 10+). Each step was motivated by real pain, not anticipated need.

This module taught the patterns for building a pipeline framework: registries for mapping component names to implementations (lesson 2), entry points for automatic plugin discovery (lesson 3), DAGs for dependency resolution and concurrent execution (lesson 4), lifecycle hooks for cross-cutting concerns like logging and metrics (lesson 5), YAML-driven pipeline definitions for configuration-driven pipeline creation (lesson 6), data contracts for schema validation between steps (lesson 7), error recovery strategies for per-step failure handling (lesson 8), and the decision framework for when a framework is justified (lesson 9). This lesson presents CelticWind's production framework as the synthesis.
The Framework Architecture

blocks.title: CelticWind pipeline framework architecture
blocks.content:
Pipeline YAML (per pipeline)
                              │
                    ┌─────────┴─────────┐
                    │  Pipeline Builder   │
                    │  (reads YAML,       │
                    │   resolves DAG,     │
                    │   validates          │
                    │   contracts)         │
                    └─────────┬─────────┘
                              │
        ┌─────────────────┴─────────────────┐
        │                                     │
┌───────┴───────┐          ┌──────────┴──────────┐
│  Registries      │          │  Step Runner + Hooks  │
│  (reader,        │          │  (logging, metrics,   │
│   validator,     │          │   retry, schema       │
│   transformer,   │          │   validation)         │
│   writer)        │          └─────────────────────┘
│  + entry points  │
└────────────────┘

Before framework:  12 pipeline scripts, 8,000 lines, 60% duplicated
After framework:   12 pipeline YAMLs + framework core (1,400 lines) + components (1,800 lines)
blocks.caption: The Pipeline Builder reads YAML definitions, resolves components from the registries (populated by entry points at startup), resolves the DAG execution order, validates data contracts between steps, and runs each step through the StepRunner with lifecycle hooks. The 12 pipelines went from 8,000 lines of code (60% duplicated) to 12 YAML files + 3,200 lines of shared code.

blocks.content: The framework reduced CelticWind's pipeline codebase from 8,000 lines (12 scripts with 60% duplication) to 3,200 lines of shared code (1,400 lines of framework core + 1,800 lines of reusable components) plus 12 YAML pipeline definitions averaging 40 lines each. The duplication dropped from 60% to near zero. Onboarding time for a new engineer dropped from 3 days (reading 12 scripts to understand the patterns) to 1 day (reading the framework documentation and 1 representative YAML file). A new pipeline from the operations team (an API-sourced telemetry pipeline) was created by writing a 35-line YAML file and a 90-line ApiTelemetryReader component — no framework modifications required.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 5 completes the architecture phase with advanced configuration management. The YAML pipeline definitions from this module contain hardcoded configuration values (max_kw: 5000, compression: snappy). Module 5 teaches pydantic-settings for typed, validated configuration that varies by environment (development, staging, production), integrates with secret management for API keys, and supports feature flags for gradual rollouts of new pipeline behavior. The framework's YAML files become templates that reference environment-specific configuration — the same pipeline definition runs in development (local files, debug logging) and production (Azure Blob, structured JSON logging) with different configuration profiles.

modules.title: Advanced Configuration Management
modules.description: Build multi-environment configuration systems using pydantic-settings for typed configuration, environment-specific profiles, secret management integration, and feature flags for gradual rollouts.

sections.title: When YAML and os.environ Are Not Enough

blocks.content: CelticWind's junior-level pipeline loaded configuration from a YAML file (Module 2 junior, lesson 6) and secrets from environment variables (Module 2 junior, lesson 7). This worked for one pipeline in one environment. At mid-level scale — 12 pipelines across development, staging, and production — the approach has three failure modes. First, the YAML has no type validation: a typo that sets max_daily_kw to 500 instead of 5000 silently passes, and the pipeline rejects 98% of valid readings. Second, there is no environment differentiation: the developer must manually switch between config.dev.yml and config.prod.yml, and a forgotten switch deploys development configuration to production. Third, secret management is manual: environment variables are set by hand on each server, with no rotation, no audit trail, and no revocation if a key is compromised.
The Three Failures

rows: YAML values are untyped strings parsed at runtime with no validation
98% of valid readings rejected. Billing team received empty Silver data for 2 days before the error was noticed.
No mechanism to detect environment mismatches. Developer forgot to change the YAML path.
Production pipeline used debug logging level, filling the 10 GB log volume in 3 hours and crashing the server.
API key leaked in git history
The key was in a .env file that was accidentally committed. Rotation was manual and took 4 hours.
EPEX SPOT API access was suspended until the key was rotated. No price data for the morning billing run.

blocks.content: Each failure has the same underlying cause: the configuration system lacks the safeguards that production systems require. Type validation catches the threshold typo at startup, before any data is processed, by rejecting values that fall outside the declared range. Environment detection prevents development configuration from reaching production by tying the configuration profile to an infrastructure-managed environment variable. Secret management integration stores keys in a vault with automated rotation and audit trails, eliminating the risk of secrets in version control. This module builds a configuration system with all three safeguards, using pydantic-settings as the foundation.

blocks.title: Configuration is infrastructure, not an afterthought
blocks.content: The junior-level track treated configuration as a convenience: a YAML file that stores paths and thresholds. At mid-level scale, configuration is infrastructure: it determines which data sources are read, which validation thresholds apply, which secrets are used, and which environment the pipeline runs in. A configuration error has the same impact as a code bug — wrong output, data loss, or pipeline failure. The configuration system deserves the same engineering rigor as the pipeline code: typed definitions, validation at load time, automated testing, and version control.

pydantic-settings extends Pydantic's BaseModel into a configuration loader that validates types, enforces constraints, applies defaults, and loads values from multiple sources (environment variables, .env files, YAML, JSON) with a single class definition. Instead of parsing YAML into an untyped dictionary and hoping the keys are correct, you define a PipelineConfig model that declares each setting with its type, default value, and constraints. If the YAML contains max_daily_kw: 500 and the model declares max_daily_kw: float = Field(ge=1000, le=10000), pydantic-settings raises a validation error at startup — before any data is processed.
Defining a Typed Configuration

from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings
from pydantic import Field
from pathlib import Path

class PathsConfig(BaseSettings):
    """File system paths for pipeline I/O."""
    bronze_dir: Path
    silver_dir: Path
    quarantine_dir: Path
    report_dir: Path
    cache_dir: Path = Path('data/cache')

class ValidationConfig(BaseSettings):
    """Validation thresholds and rules."""
    min_daily_kw: float = Field(default=0.0, ge=0.0)
    max_daily_kw: float = Field(default=5000.0, ge=1000.0, le=10000.0)
    max_rejection_rate: float = Field(default=0.01, ge=0.0, le=1.0)
    valid_regions: set[str] = {'DUBLIN-NORTH', 'DUBLIN-SOUTH', 'CORK-EAST', 'CORK-WEST',
                                'GALWAY-COAST', 'GALWAY-INLAND', 'BELFAST-EAST', 'BELFAST-WEST'}

class PipelineConfig(BaseSettings):
    """Top-level pipeline configuration."""
    model_config = {'env_prefix': 'CELTICWIND_'}  # loads from CELTICWIND_* env vars

    pipeline_name: str
    environment: str = Field(default='development', pattern='^(development|staging|production)$')
    log_level: str = Field(default='INFO', pattern='^(DEBUG|INFO|WARNING|ERROR)$')
    chunk_size: int = Field(default=50000, ge=1000, le=500000)
    paths: PathsConfig
    validation: ValidationConfig

Each field declares its type (float, Path, str, set[str]), a default value where appropriate, and constraints (ge for minimum, le for maximum, pattern for regex validation). The environment field accepts only 'development', 'staging', or 'production' — any other value raises a validation error at load time. The max_daily_kw field has a minimum of 1000, which prevents the threshold typo from lesson 1 (a value of 500 would be rejected immediately). The env_prefix configuration means environment variables like CELTICWIND_PIPELINE_NAME and CELTICWIND_LOG_LEVEL are automatically loaded without explicit os.environ calls in the application code.
Loading and Validating Configuration

import yaml
from pathlib import Path

def load_config(yaml_path: Path) -> PipelineConfig:
    """Load configuration from YAML with pydantic-settings validation."""
    with open(yaml_path) as f:
        raw = yaml.safe_load(f)

    # pydantic-settings validates types, constraints, and required fields
    config = PipelineConfig(**raw)
    return config

# Success: all fields valid
config = load_config(Path('config/config.yml'))
print(config.validation.max_daily_kw)  # 5000.0 (float, validated)
print(config.paths.bronze_dir)         # PosixPath('data/bronze') (Path object)

# Failure: max_daily_kw = 500 is below the minimum of 1000
# ValidationError: 1 validation error for PipelineConfig
# validation -> max_daily_kw
#   Input should be >= 1000 [type=greater_than_equal]

The load_config function reads the YAML into a raw dictionary and passes it to PipelineConfig, which validates every field. If validation fails, pydantic-settings raises a ValidationError with the specific field, the constraint that was violated, and the invalid value — a clear diagnosis that points directly to the configuration error. At CelticWind, load_config is the first function called in the pipeline's entry point. If configuration validation fails, the pipeline exits immediately with exit code 3 (configuration error), and the monitoring system alerts the on-call engineer with the validation error message.

blocks.title: The configuration model is the documentation
blocks.content: Reading PipelineConfig tells you everything about the pipeline's configuration: which fields exist, what types they are, what values are valid, and what defaults apply. When a new engineer asks 'what does max_daily_kw do and what values are acceptable?', the answer is in the Field definition: ge=1000, le=10000. This is the same principle as Pydantic validation models in Module 8 junior: the model is both the validation logic and the schema documentation.

Production configuration comes from multiple sources: the YAML file provides default values and environment-specific settings, environment variables provide secrets and runtime overrides, .env files provide local development settings that are not committed to version control, and command-line arguments provide one-off overrides for debugging and incident response. When the same setting appears in multiple sources (CELTICWIND_LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG in the environment and log_level: INFO in the YAML), one source must take precedence over the others. pydantic-settings resolves this with a defined hierarchy: command-line arguments override environment variables, which override .env files, which override YAML defaults.
The Precedence Hierarchy

blocks.title: Configuration source precedence (highest to lowest)
blocks.content:
Priority   Source                 Example
  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  1 (highest) CLI arguments          --log-level DEBUG
  2           Environment variables   CELTICWIND_LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG
  3           .env file              CELTICWIND_LOG_LEVEL=WARNING
  4 (lowest)  YAML config file       log_level: INFO
  5           Model defaults         log_level: str = 'INFO'

  Resolution: CLI > env var > .env > YAML > default
  Result for log_level: DEBUG (from CLI, highest priority)
blocks.caption: When a setting appears in multiple sources, the highest-priority source wins. CLI arguments override everything, environment variables override file-based sources, and YAML values override model defaults. This hierarchy enables environment-specific overrides without modifying configuration files.

blocks.content: Implementing Multi-Source Loading

import yaml
import argparse
from pathlib import Path
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings

def load_config_with_precedence(
    yaml_path: Path,
    cli_overrides: dict | None = None,
) -> PipelineConfig:
    """Load configuration with defined precedence:
    CLI > env vars > .env > YAML > defaults.
    """
    # Layer 1: YAML file (lowest file-based priority)
    with open(yaml_path) as f:
        yaml_values = yaml.safe_load(f) or {}

    # Layer 2: CLI overrides (highest priority, applied on top)
    if cli_overrides:
        yaml_values = deep_merge(yaml_values, cli_overrides)

    # pydantic-settings automatically layers:
    # - Model defaults (lowest)
    # - yaml_values dict (passed as **kwargs)
    # - .env file (if present, loaded by BaseSettings)
    # - Environment variables (CELTICWIND_* prefix, loaded by BaseSettings)
    config = PipelineConfig(**yaml_values)
    return config

def parse_cli_args() -> dict:
    """Parse command-line overrides."""
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument('--log-level', dest='log_level')
    parser.add_argument('--chunk-size', dest='chunk_size', type=int)
    args = parser.parse_args()
    return {k: v for k, v in vars(args).items() if v is not None}

The loading function applies sources in order: YAML values form the base, pydantic-settings overlays .env file values and environment variables automatically (through the BaseSettings mechanism), and CLI overrides are merged on top. The deep_merge function recursively merges nested dictionaries so that a CLI override of --chunk-size 10000 replaces only that field without affecting the rest of the configuration. At CelticWind, the standard invocation is python -m celticwind.pipeline config/production.yml — the YAML provides all settings, and environment variables provide secrets. CLI overrides are used only during debugging: python -m celticwind.pipeline config/production.yml --log-level DEBUG --chunk-size 1000.

blocks.title: Environment variables override YAML silently
blocks.content: A stale CELTICWIND_LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG environment variable on the production server overrides the YAML's log_level: INFO without any warning. The pipeline runs with debug logging, filling the log volume. At CelticWind, the pipeline logs the effective configuration (with secrets masked) at startup: logger.info('Effective config: %s', config.model_dump(exclude={'api_keys'})). This log entry makes silent overrides visible: if the log shows log_level=DEBUG when the YAML says INFO, the engineer knows an environment variable is active.

CelticWind's pipeline runs in three environments: development (local laptop, small test data, debug logging), staging (staging server, production-scale data, info logging), and production (production server, real data, structured JSON logging with alerting). Each environment needs different paths, different thresholds (development uses lenient thresholds for faster iteration), different logging configurations, and different secret sources. The junior-level approach — three separate YAML files selected manually — works but is error-prone: the developer must remember to switch the file path, and a wrong switch deploys development settings to production.
Environment Detection and Profile Loading

import os
from pathlib import Path
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def detect_environment() -> str:
    """Detect the current environment from CELTICWIND_ENV."""
    env = os.environ.get('CELTICWIND_ENV', 'development').lower()
    valid_envs = {'development', 'staging', 'production'}
    if env not in valid_envs:
        raise ValueError(f'Invalid CELTICWIND_ENV={env!r}. Must be one of: {valid_envs}')
    return env

def load_environment_config(config_dir: Path) -> PipelineConfig:
    """Load the configuration profile for the current environment."""
    env = detect_environment()
    base_path = config_dir / 'base.yml'
    env_path = config_dir / f'{env}.yml'

    # Load base config (shared defaults)
    base = yaml.safe_load(base_path.read_text()) or {}

    # Overlay environment-specific values
    if env_path.exists():
        env_values = yaml.safe_load(env_path.read_text()) or {}
        config_values = deep_merge(base, env_values)
    else:
        config_values = base

    config = PipelineConfig(**config_values)
    logger.info('Loaded %s configuration from %s', env, config_dir)
    return config

# config/
#   base.yml          — shared defaults (chunk_size, valid_regions)
#   development.yml   — local paths, debug logging, lenient thresholds
#   staging.yml       — staging paths, info logging, production thresholds
#   production.yml    — production paths, JSON logging, strict thresholds

The base.yml file contains settings shared across all environments (valid_regions, chunk_size defaults, pipeline names). Each environment-specific file overrides only the settings that differ: development.yml sets paths to local directories and logging to DEBUG, production.yml sets paths to Azure Blob endpoints and logging to structured JSON. The deep_merge function ensures that environment-specific values override base values without erasing unrelated settings. The CELTICWIND_ENV environment variable is set by the deployment system (Kubernetes, Docker Compose, CI) — not by the developer manually, eliminating the risk of deploying the wrong profile.
Environment-Specific Validation

Some configuration constraints differ between environments. Development allows max_daily_kw thresholds as low as 100 (for testing with synthetic data). Production requires max_daily_kw to be at least 1000 (to prevent threshold typos from rejecting valid data). This conditional validation is implemented as a model validator in pydantic-settings: if environment == 'production' and max_daily_kw < 1000, raise a validation error. At CelticWind, the production profile has 4 additional constraints: log_level must be INFO or higher (no DEBUG in production), chunk_size must be at least 10000 (small chunks slow the production pipeline), paths must use the Azure Blob prefix (no local paths in production), and the secret source must be 'azure_keyvault' (no .env files in production).

blocks.title: The environment should be detected, not configured
blocks.content: The CELTICWIND_ENV variable is set by the infrastructure (the Kubernetes pod spec, the Docker Compose environment block, the CI pipeline step), not by the application code or the YAML files. This means the application cannot accidentally override its own environment detection. At CelticWind, the production Kubernetes deployment sets CELTICWIND_ENV=production in the pod spec, and the pipeline's configuration loader trusts this value absolutely. If the variable is missing, the default is 'development' — the safest fallback because development settings cannot damage production data.

The junior-level track stored secrets in environment variables (CELTICWIND_EPEX_API_KEY) and .env files. This works for development but has three production limitations. First, environment variables are visible to any process on the server (ps aux shows them, /proc/pid/environ exposes them). Second, rotation requires restarting the pipeline to pick up the new value. Third, there is no audit trail — no record of who accessed the secret or when. Secret management services (Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) solve all three: secrets are stored encrypted, accessed via authenticated API calls (with audit logs), and can be rotated without restarting the consumer.
The SecretsProvider Protocol

The SecretsProvider protocol (from Module 1's protocol pattern) defines a single method: get_secret(key) -> str. The EnvironmentSecretsProvider reads secrets from environment variables with a prefix — identical to the junior-level approach, now wrapped in a protocol. The AzureKeyVaultProvider reads secrets from Azure Key Vault using the Azure SDK, with audit-logged access and automatic credential management via DefaultAzureCredential. The pipeline receives a SecretsProvider through dependency injection and calls provider.get_secret('EPEX_API_KEY') without knowing whether the secret comes from an environment variable or a vault.
Injecting Secrets into Configuration

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ApiSecrets:
    """API credentials loaded from the secrets provider."""
    epex_api_key: str
    weather_api_key: str

def load_secrets(provider: SecretsProvider) -> ApiSecrets:
    """Load all pipeline secrets from the configured provider."""
    return ApiSecrets(
        epex_api_key=provider.get_secret('EPEX_API_KEY'),
        weather_api_key=provider.get_secret('WEATHER_API_KEY'),
    )

# Development: secrets from environment variables
dev_provider = EnvironmentSecretsProvider(prefix='CELTICWIND_')
secrets = load_secrets(dev_provider)

# Production: secrets from Azure Key Vault
prod_provider = AzureKeyVaultProvider(vault_url='https://celticwind.vault.azure.net/')
secrets = load_secrets(prod_provider)

The load_secrets function requests all secrets upfront at pipeline startup — not lazily during execution. If a secret is missing, the pipeline fails immediately with a clear KeyError instead of crashing mid-execution when the API client first needs the key. The ApiSecrets dataclass groups all secrets into a typed container, preventing the scattered os.environ.get('KEY') calls that the junior-level track used. At CelticWind, the secrets provider is selected based on the environment: EnvironmentSecretsProvider for development and staging (where Key Vault access may not be configured), AzureKeyVaultProvider for production.

blocks.title: Never log secret values
blocks.content: The AzureKeyVaultProvider logs the secret name and version but never the value. A logger.info('Loaded API key: %s', secret.value) line would write the secret to the log file in plaintext. At CelticWind, the logging configuration includes a SecretMaskingFilter that redacts any string matching known secret patterns (API keys, tokens, passwords) from log output. The filter is applied to the root logger so that no handler can accidentally emit unmasked secrets — even if a developer accidentally logs a secret value.

CelticWind is adding a new sub-region (LIMERICK) to the pipeline. The new region's meter data has slightly different characteristics (higher average consumption, different tariff structure) that may trigger unexpected validation rejections. Deploying the LIMERICK support directly to production risks disrupting the 8 existing regions if the new code has a bug. Feature flags provide a safer alternative: the LIMERICK processing is developed behind a flag (enable_limerick_region), tested in staging with the flag enabled, and rolled out to production with the flag initially disabled. The operations team enables the flag for 10% of pipeline runs, monitors the results, and gradually increases to 100% once confidence is established.
A Simple Feature Flag System

from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings
from pydantic import Field
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class FeatureFlags(BaseSettings):
    """Feature flags for gradual rollout of new pipeline behavior."""
    model_config = {'env_prefix': 'CELTICWIND_FLAG_'}

    enable_limerick_region: bool = False
    use_polars_transformer: bool = False
    enable_price_enrichment: bool = True
    enable_concurrent_reads: bool = True
    validation_strategy: str = Field(default='strict', pattern='^(strict|lenient|experimental)$')

    def log_active_flags(self) -> None:
        """Log all flag values for audit and debugging."""
        for field_name, value in self.model_dump().items():
            logger.info('Feature flag: %s=%s', field_name, value)

# Usage in the pipeline
def process_meter_readings(readings: list[dict], flags: FeatureFlags) -> list[dict]:
    # Filter regions based on feature flag
    valid_regions = {'DUBLIN-NORTH', 'DUBLIN-SOUTH', 'CORK-EAST', 'CORK-WEST',
                     'GALWAY-COAST', 'GALWAY-INLAND', 'BELFAST-EAST', 'BELFAST-WEST'}
    if flags.enable_limerick_region:
        valid_regions.add('LIMERICK')

    return [r for r in readings if r.get('region', '').upper() in valid_regions]

Each feature flag is a typed field with a default value. The env_prefix means CELTICWIND_FLAG_ENABLE_LIMERICK_REGION=true in the environment enables the flag without modifying the YAML file. The log_active_flags method records every flag's value at pipeline startup, creating an audit trail: if a pipeline run produces unexpected results, the engineer checks which flags were active during that run. At CelticWind, feature flags are the standard mechanism for deploying new behavior: code is merged behind a flag, tested in staging with the flag enabled, and gradually rolled out to production.
The Gradual Rollout Pattern

The operations team enables a new flag by setting the environment variable in the production deployment configuration (the Kubernetes pod spec or Docker Compose file). For the first week, the flag is enabled on only the BELFAST-EAST sub-region's pipeline run (a low-volume region where the blast radius of a bug is small). If the BELFAST-EAST run produces correct results for 7 days, the flag is enabled for all regions. This progressive rollout catches region-specific bugs (the LIMERICK tariff structure triggers a validation edge case) before they affect high-volume regions like DUBLIN-NORTH. At CelticWind, the feature flag lifecycle is: code merged (flag off) → tested in staging (flag on) → canary in production (flag on for 1 region) → full rollout (flag on for all regions) → flag removed from code (behavior is permanent).

blocks.title: Remove flags after full rollout
blocks.content: Feature flags that are permanently enabled are dead code: they add conditional logic that is never exercised in the 'off' path. At CelticWind, every feature flag has a removal date in the YAML documentation. After the flag has been fully rolled out and stable for 30 days, it is removed from the FeatureFlags model and the conditional code is simplified. The code review checklist includes: 'Are there any feature flags older than 90 days that should be cleaned up?' Stale flags accumulate technical debt.

A configuration error that reaches production causes the same damage as a code bug: wrong output, pipeline failure, or data corruption. Configuration testing prevents this by validating every environment's configuration file in CI — before the configuration reaches any server. At CelticWind, the CI pipeline loads each environment's YAML file through the PipelineConfig model and verifies that it passes validation. If the staging YAML has a typo (chukn_size instead of chunk_size), the CI build fails with a clear validation error message, and the pull request cannot be merged.
Testing Configuration Files in CI

The parametrized test loads each environment's configuration and verifies that it passes pydantic-settings validation. The production-specific test enforces stricter constraints that apply only in production (no DEBUG logging, minimum threshold values, minimum chunk sizes). The key consistency test catches a common configuration error: a new setting added to development.yml but forgotten in production.yml. At CelticWind, these tests run on every pull request that modifies any file in the config/ directory, catching configuration errors at the same stage as code errors.
Testing Configuration Behavior

Configuration validation tests verify that the YAML files are well-formed. Behavior tests verify that the configuration produces the expected pipeline behavior: a test that loads the staging configuration and runs the pipeline on synthetic data, verifying that the staging thresholds produce the expected rejection rate. At CelticWind, the CI pipeline includes a config-smoke-test step that loads each environment's configuration, creates a pipeline from it (using the framework from Module 4), runs it on a 1,000-row synthetic dataset, and asserts on the output schema and rejection rate. This catches not just typos but semantic errors: a max_daily_kw of 5000 in the YAML that should be 50 for a specific pipeline (a correct value for the wrong pipeline).

blocks.title: Configuration tests are the cheapest production protection
blocks.content: A configuration test takes seconds to write, runs in milliseconds, and catches errors that would cause hours of production downtime. The cost-benefit ratio is among the highest of any engineering practice. At CelticWind, the team estimates that configuration tests have prevented 4 production incidents in the first 6 months — each of which would have required at least 2 hours of engineer time to diagnose and fix.

Configuration drift occurs when the active configuration on a server diverges from the committed configuration in version control. This happens when an engineer modifies a YAML file directly on the server (bypassing the deployment pipeline), when an environment variable is set manually and forgotten, or when a deployment partially fails (the new code is deployed but the new configuration is not). Drift is dangerous because the pipeline runs with configuration that was never reviewed, tested, or approved — and the discrepancy is invisible until something goes wrong.
Detecting Drift at Pipeline Startup

import hashlib
import json
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def compute_config_hash(config: PipelineConfig) -> str:
    """Compute a deterministic hash of the effective configuration."""
    config_dict = config.model_dump(mode='json')
    config_json = json.dumps(config_dict, sort_keys=True, default=str)
    return hashlib.sha256(config_json.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

def check_config_drift(
    config: PipelineConfig,
    expected_hash: str | None,
) -> None:
    """Compare the active config hash to the expected hash."""
    actual_hash = compute_config_hash(config)
    logger.info('Config hash: %s (expected: %s)', actual_hash, expected_hash or 'none')

    if expected_hash and actual_hash != expected_hash:
        logger.warning(
            'Configuration drift detected: expected hash %s, got %s. '
            'Active config may differ from the committed version.',
            expected_hash, actual_hash,
        )

# The expected hash is stored in the deployment manifest
# and compared at every pipeline startup

The configuration hash is a deterministic fingerprint of the effective configuration (all sources merged). The deployment pipeline computes the hash from the committed configuration files and stores it in the deployment manifest. At each pipeline startup, the pipeline computes the hash of the active configuration and compares it to the expected hash. If they differ, a drift warning is logged. At CelticWind, drift detection runs at the INFO log level — it does not prevent the pipeline from running (because the drift might be intentional, such as an emergency environment variable override) but makes the discrepancy visible to the monitoring system.
Scheduled Drift Audits

Startup drift detection catches drift on every pipeline run. Scheduled drift audits catch drift between runs: a weekly job that connects to each production server, reads the active configuration, and compares it to the committed version in git. At CelticWind, the drift audit runs every Sunday at 10:00 and produces a report listing any servers where the active configuration differs from the committed version. The report is sent to the platform team's Slack channel. The most common drift causes are emergency environment variable overrides that were not reverted after the incident, and manual YAML edits made during debugging sessions that were forgotten.

blocks.title: Drift detection detects, it does not prevent
blocks.content: The drift detection system warns that the active configuration differs from the committed version. It does not prevent the pipeline from running with the drifted configuration because some drift is intentional (emergency overrides during incidents). At CelticWind, the policy is: every drift warning must be investigated within 48 hours. If the drift is intentional, the committed configuration is updated to match. If the drift is accidental, the server's configuration is corrected. The goal is zero drift at the end of each week, not zero drift at every moment.

This module has built a substantial configuration system: typed models, multi-source loading, environment profiles, secret management, feature flags, configuration testing, and drift detection. The temptation is to make everything configurable: every threshold, every SQL query, every file path, every retry count, every log message format. Over-configuration creates a meta-programming problem where the YAML file becomes a programming language — without a debugger, type checker, test framework, or version control diff that shows semantic changes. The YAML grows to hundreds of lines, changes to one setting have unexpected effects on another, and debugging requires understanding both the code and the configuration simultaneously.
The Configuration Decision Framework

rows: The value changes between environments (paths, endpoints, log levels)
The value is the same in every environment (the Parquet compression algorithm, the CSV encoding)
The value changes without a code change (thresholds adjusted by operators)
The value changes only when the code changes (a SQL query, a transformation function)
The value is a secret (API keys, database passwords)
The value is a constant that has no security sensitivity (the pipeline name)
The value controls behavior that operators need to adjust during incidents (feature flags, retry counts)
The value is a design decision that should not change without code review (the data model schema)

blocks.content: The table distills the principle: configure values that vary independently of the code. If a value changes only when the code changes (the SQL query that the pipeline runs, the Parquet schema it writes), it belongs in the code — where it benefits from version control, code review, type checking, and test coverage. If a value changes between environments or between deployments without a code change (paths, thresholds, secrets, feature flags), it belongs in configuration. At CelticWind, the rule is: if changing the value requires understanding the code to assess the impact, it belongs in the code. If the value can be changed safely by an operator without reading the code, it belongs in configuration.
Signs of Over-Configuration

Three signals indicate over-configuration. First, the YAML file exceeds 200 lines: the configuration has become a specification document rather than a set of environment-specific values. Second, changes to the YAML require code changes to take effect: the configuration and the code are coupled, defeating the purpose of externalizing the value. Third, the team cannot explain what a configuration setting does without reading the code: the setting is a code concept exposed as configuration without sufficient abstraction. At CelticWind, the configuration review checklist asks: 'If I change this value, can I predict the impact without reading the code?' If the answer is no, the value should be in the code, not the configuration.

blocks.title: The best configuration is the smallest configuration
blocks.content: Every configurable value is a decision that the operator must understand. A pipeline with 15 settings is comprehensible; a pipeline with 150 settings is intimidating and error-prone. At CelticWind, the PipelineConfig model has 22 settings (12 paths, 4 validation thresholds, 3 processing parameters, and 3 operational settings). The team periodically reviews the configuration model and removes settings that have the same value in every environment — those are defaults that belong in the code, not settings that belong in the configuration.

This module taught the configuration system that production pipelines need: pydantic-settings for typed, validated configuration (lesson 2), multi-source loading with defined precedence (lesson 3), environment profiles for dev/staging/production differentiation (lesson 4), secret management with the SecretsProvider protocol and Azure Key Vault integration (lesson 5), feature flags for gradual rollouts (lesson 6), configuration testing in CI (lesson 7), drift detection at startup and via scheduled audits (lesson 8), and the discipline of configuring only what needs to be configurable (lesson 9). This lesson presents CelticWind's production configuration architecture as the synthesis.
The Configuration Architecture

blocks.title: CelticWind configuration loading and validation flow
blocks.caption: Configuration flows from four sources (YAML files, environment variables, Key Vault) through pydantic-settings validation into a typed PipelineConfig and ApiSecrets. The Pipeline Factory creates the pipeline from the validated configuration, logs the configuration hash for drift detection, and records the active feature flags for audit.

blocks.content: The architecture follows a three-step flow at pipeline startup. First, configuration loading: base.yml and the environment-specific YAML are merged, environment variables are overlaid by pydantic-settings, and secrets are loaded from the SecretsProvider. Second, validation: PipelineConfig validates types, constraints, and environment-specific rules. Third, audit: the configuration hash is computed and compared to the expected value for drift detection, feature flags are logged for the audit trail, and the effective configuration (with secrets masked) is logged for debugging. If any step fails, the pipeline exits with exit code 3 before processing any data.

The architecture phase (Modules 4–5) is complete. Module 4 built the pipeline framework that eliminates duplication across 12 pipelines: registries, entry points, DAGs, hooks, and YAML-driven definitions. Module 5 built the configuration system that makes the framework portable across environments: typed validation, multi-source loading, secret management, feature flags, and drift detection. Together, they transform the junior-level collection of scripts into a mid-level platform that 3 teams can operate independently. The next phase (Modules 6–7) adds engineering discipline: advanced testing patterns that verify the framework and configuration behave correctly, and CI/CD automation that deploys them safely.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 6 builds on the configuration testing from this module with advanced testing patterns: property-based testing that generates random valid and invalid configurations to verify the validation rules, snapshot testing that detects unintentional changes to the pipeline's output format, and integration tests that exercise the entire pipeline from YAML configuration through data processing to Parquet output. The configuration system from Module 5 makes integration testing practical: the test uses the development profile, overrides specific settings with CLI arguments, and runs the pipeline on synthetic data — all managed by the configuration system.

modules.title: Advanced Testing Patterns
modules.description: Write advanced test suites with property-based testing, snapshot testing, integration test infrastructure, test data factories, and performance regression detection.

blocks.content: Module 10 junior built CelticWind's test suite with pytest: unit tests for individual functions (validate_reading, normalize_region), parametrized tests for validation rules, fixtures for shared test data, mocks for external dependencies, and CI integration. That suite verifies specific input-output pairs — the 8 parametrized validation cases, the 3 reader scenarios, the 2 writer roundtrip tests. It catches bugs that produce wrong results for those specific inputs. But it misses bugs that surface with inputs the developer did not anticipate: a meter_id that contains Unicode characters, a daily_kw at the exact boundary value, or a date string with an unexpected timezone offset. Mid-level testing adds three dimensions that close these gaps.
The Three Dimensions

rows: Tests use 8-10 handcrafted inputs that the developer chose
Property-based testing generates 100+ random inputs that satisfy constraints
Edge cases the developer did not anticipate: Unicode meter IDs, boundary values, empty strings with whitespace
Snapshot testing captures the complete output and detects any change
Unintentional changes to output format, field ordering, or derived values that existing assertions do not cover
Tests verify individual functions in isolation with mock data
Integration tests run the full pipeline from CSV to Parquet on realistic synthetic data
Wiring bugs: components that pass unit tests individually but fail when composed (schema mismatches, type conversion losses)

blocks.content: Each dimension addresses a different failure mode. Property-based testing catches bugs in the input handling logic (the validator accepts a meter_id with embedded newlines because no handcrafted test included one). Snapshot testing catches bugs in the output format (a refactor that changes the validation report's field ordering breaks a downstream consumer that parses by position). Integration testing catches bugs in the composition (the Polars transformer produces float64 values but the Parquet writer expects float32, producing silent precision loss). At CelticWind, the mid-level test suite combines all three dimensions with the junior-level unit tests, reaching 94% code coverage with 280 tests that run in 12 seconds.

blocks.title: Testing at mid-level is about coverage of the unexpected
blocks.content: Junior-level tests verify known behaviors with known inputs. Mid-level tests verify invariants across unknown inputs (property-based), detect unknown changes in output (snapshot), and verify unknown interactions between components (integration). The shift is from 'does the function produce the right answer for these specific inputs?' to 'does the function behave correctly for any valid input, and does the system produce consistent output when components are composed?'

The Hypothesis library generates random test inputs that satisfy declared constraints and verifies that a function's invariants hold for every generated input. Instead of testing validate_reading with 8 specific dictionaries, Hypothesis generates 100 random dictionaries with realistic field values and verifies three invariants: every valid reading has daily_kw ≥ 0, every rejected reading has a documented reason, and the total of valid plus rejected equals the input count. If any invariant fails, Hypothesis reports the minimal failing input — often revealing an edge case that no handcrafted test would have included.
A Property-Based Validation Test

from hypothesis import given, settings
from hypothesis import strategies as st

@given(
    meter_id=st.text(min_size=1, max_size=20),
    daily_kw=st.floats(min_value=-100, max_value=10000, allow_nan=False, allow_infinity=False),
    date=st.dates().map(lambda d: d.isoformat()),
    region=st.sampled_from(['DUBLIN-NORTH', 'CORK-EAST', 'GALWAY-COAST', 'BELFAST-WEST', 'INVALID']),
)
@settings(max_examples=200)
def test_validation_invariants(meter_id, daily_kw, date, region):
    """Property: validation always partitions input into valid + rejected."""
    raw = {
        'meter_id': meter_id,
        'daily_kw': str(daily_kw),
        'date': date,
        'region': region,
        'customer_type': 'RESIDENTIAL',
    }

    valid, rejected = validate_with_quarantine([raw])

    # Invariant 1: total is preserved
    assert len(valid) + len(rejected) == 1

    # Invariant 2: valid readings have non-negative daily_kw
    for v in valid:
        assert float(v['daily_kw']) >= 0

    # Invariant 3: rejected readings have a reason
    for r in rejected:
        assert '_quarantine_reasons' in r
        assert len(r['_quarantine_reasons']) > 0

The @given decorator tells Hypothesis to generate random values for each parameter: meter_id is a random string (1-20 characters), daily_kw is a random float (-100 to 10000), date is a random valid date, and region is sampled from a list that includes one invalid value. Hypothesis runs the test 200 times with different random inputs and verifies the three invariants after each run. The invariants are universal properties — they must hold for every possible input, not just the specific inputs a developer would choose. At CelticWind, this test found a bug in the validation function: a daily_kw of exactly 0.0 was being rejected because the range check used > instead of >=.
How Hypothesis Finds Bugs

When an invariant fails, Hypothesis does not just report the failing input — it shrinks the input to the minimal failing example. If the test fails for meter_id='IE-DUB-00142
' (a meter ID with an embedded newline), Hypothesis tries simpler inputs ('a
', '
') until it finds the simplest input that still fails. The shrunk example is easier to diagnose: '
' immediately reveals that the bug is about whitespace handling, not about the specific meter ID format. At CelticWind, Hypothesis found 3 bugs in the first week: the 0.0 boundary check, a meter_id with leading whitespace that passed validation but caused a KeyError in the transformer, and a date string with timezone offset ('2025-01-15+01:00') that fromisoformat parsed differently than expected.

blocks.title: Test invariants, not specific outputs
blocks.content: Property-based tests verify universal properties (the output partition preserves the input count, valid readings meet constraints, rejected readings have reasons) rather than specific outputs (this meter produces this exact result). This is a different testing mindset: instead of asking 'what is the correct output for this input?' you ask 'what must be true about the output for any valid input?' The invariant-based approach is complementary to example-based tests — use both in the same suite.

Lesson 2's test used generic strategies (st.text, st.floats) that generate any string or float within bounds. This produces inputs that are valid Python values but may not be realistic CelticWind data: meter IDs like '\x00\xff' or dates in the year 9999. Custom strategies generate data that matches the CelticWind domain: meter IDs following the IE-XXX-NNNNN pattern, daily_kw values in the realistic range for residential meters, and dates within the last year. Custom strategies are reusable — the same meter_readings() strategy serves validation tests, transformation tests, and aggregation tests, replacing the hardcoded fixture data from the junior-level conftest.py.
Building CelticWind Strategies

from hypothesis import strategies as st
from datetime import date, timedelta

# Strategy for a valid CelticWind meter ID
meter_id_strategy = st.from_regex(
    r'IE-(DUB|CRK|GAL|BEL)-(N|S|E|W)-[0-9]{5}', fullmatch=True
)

# Strategy for a realistic daily_kw value
daily_kw_strategy = st.floats(min_value=0.0, max_value=200.0, allow_nan=False)

# Strategy for a recent date string
recent_date_strategy = st.dates(
    min_value=date.today() - timedelta(days=365),
    max_value=date.today(),
).map(lambda d: d.isoformat())

# Strategy for a valid region
region_strategy = st.sampled_from([
    'DUBLIN-NORTH', 'DUBLIN-SOUTH', 'CORK-EAST', 'CORK-WEST',
    'GALWAY-COAST', 'GALWAY-INLAND', 'BELFAST-EAST', 'BELFAST-WEST',
])

# Composite strategy: a complete valid meter reading
@st.composite
def valid_meter_reading(draw):
    return {
        'meter_id': draw(meter_id_strategy),
        'daily_kw': str(draw(daily_kw_strategy)),
        'date': draw(recent_date_strategy),
        'region': draw(region_strategy),
        'customer_type': draw(st.sampled_from(['RESIDENTIAL', 'COMMERCIAL', 'INDUSTRIAL'])),
    }

# Strategy: a batch of valid readings
valid_readings_batch = st.lists(valid_meter_reading(), min_size=1, max_size=100)

The @st.composite decorator builds a strategy from other strategies using the draw function. The valid_meter_reading strategy generates a dictionary with realistic field values: a meter ID matching the CelticWind naming convention, a daily_kw in the residential range (0-200), a date within the last year, and a valid region. The valid_readings_batch strategy generates lists of 1-100 such readings. These strategies replace the hardcoded 5-reading fixture from Module 10 junior with a generator that produces different data on every test run — increasing the chance of discovering edge-case bugs.
Strategies for Invalid Data

The invalid_meter_reading strategy starts with a valid reading and corrupts exactly one field, testing the validation function's ability to detect each invalid dimension independently. The property test verifies that every invalid reading is rejected with a documented reason — regardless of which specific field is invalid.
