blocks.content: If the Silver write is not idempotent (lesson 2), reprocessing a date appends new data alongside the existing output instead of replacing it. If the reprocessing job fails midway, some dates have the old output, some have the new output, and some have both. At CelticWind, the reprocessing runbook's first step is: 'Verify that the pipeline uses idempotent writes (atomic overwrite per partition). If not, do not reprocess — escalate to the platform team.'

An SLA (Service Level Agreement) is a commitment from the data engineering team to its consumers: the Silver data will be available by 06:00 UTC, at least 99.9% of expected records will be present, and the rejection rate will be below 1%. Without a formal SLA, the billing team's expectation is 'the data should be there when I need it' — a vague standard that is impossible to meet consistently. With a formal SLA, both sides agree on specific, measurable targets. The engineering team knows what to optimize for (freshness, completeness, quality), and the billing team knows what to expect (and when to escalate if expectations are not met).
CelticWind's SLA Dimensions

rows: Not completed by 05:00 (1 hour buffer for investigation)

blocks.content: Each dimension has a target (the commitment), a measurement (the metric that tracks it), and an alert threshold (the point at which the engineering team investigates before the SLA is breached). The alert thresholds are set inside the SLA boundaries: the freshness alert fires at 05:00, providing 1 hour of investigation time before the 06:00 SLA deadline. The completeness alert fires at 99.5%, providing a 0.4% buffer before the 99.9% SLA boundary. At CelticWind, the SLA dashboard (part of the Grafana overview from Module 9) shows a 30-day rolling view of each dimension, with green/yellow/red status based on the proximity to the SLA boundary.
SLA Reporting to Consumers

The SLA is only meaningful if consumers can verify it. At CelticWind, the weekly data quality report (emailed to the billing, analytics, and operations teams) includes the SLA compliance for each dimension over the past 7 days: 7/7 runs completed by 06:00, completeness averaged 99.97%, rejection rate averaged 0.04%, and availability was 100%. If any dimension breached its target, the report includes the incident summary and the remediation taken. This transparency builds trust: the consumers see that the engineering team measures, monitors, and responds to quality issues — not just claims that 'the data is reliable' without evidence.

blocks.title: SLAs should be negotiated, not dictated
blocks.content: A 06:00 freshness SLA is meaningless if the billing team actually needs the data by 04:00 for their own processing deadline. An SLA that the engineering team defines unilaterally may not match the consumer's actual needs. At CelticWind, SLAs are negotiated in quarterly planning meetings between the engineering team and each consuming team. The billing team's platinum SLA (06:00 freshness, 99.9% completeness) reflects their invoicing deadline. The analytics team's gold SLA (09:00 freshness, 99% completeness) reflects their less urgent exploratory workload.

CelticWind's 1.2 million meters are growing at 15% annually as new wind farms and tidal installations come online. At this rate, the meter count reaches 1.38 million next year, 1.59 million in year two, and 2.38 million in year five. The Silver pipeline currently takes 63 seconds with a 300-second SLA — 79% headroom. But runtime does not grow linearly with data: some operations scale as O(n) (reading, writing), some scale as O(n log n) (sorting), and some may have hidden O(n²) components that only surface at larger scale. Capacity planning projects when the pipeline will breach its SLA under realistic growth assumptions and identifies the infrastructure changes needed before that point.
Projecting Pipeline Runtime Growth

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class CapacityProjection:
    """Project pipeline runtime at future data volumes."""
    current_rows: int
    current_runtime_s: float
    sla_seconds: float
    annual_growth_rate: float

    def project(self, years: int) -> list[dict]:
        projections = []
        rows = self.current_rows
        for year in range(1, years + 1):
            rows = int(rows * (1 + self.annual_growth_rate))
            # Assume linear scaling (conservative; may be worse)
            runtime = self.current_runtime_s * (rows / self.current_rows)
            headroom = 1 - (runtime / self.sla_seconds)
            projections.append({
                'year': year, 'rows': rows,
                'projected_runtime_s': round(runtime, 1),
                'sla_headroom_pct': round(headroom * 100, 1),
                'action_needed': headroom < 0.2,  # < 20% headroom
            })
        return projections

proj = CapacityProjection(
    current_rows=1_200_000, current_runtime_s=63,
    sla_seconds=300, annual_growth_rate=0.15,
)
for p in proj.project(5):
    print(f"Year {p['year']}: {p['rows']:,} rows, {p['projected_runtime_s']}s, "
          f"headroom: {p['sla_headroom_pct']}%, action: {p['action_needed']}")
# Year 1: 1,380,000 rows, 72.5s, headroom: 75.8%, action: False
# Year 2: 1,587,000 rows, 83.3s, headroom: 72.2%, action: False
# Year 3: 1,825,050 rows, 95.8s, headroom: 68.1%, action: False
# Year 4: 2,098,808 rows, 110.2s, headroom: 63.3%, action: False
# Year 5: 2,413,629 rows, 126.7s, headroom: 57.8%, action: False

The projection assumes linear runtime scaling — the conservative case. If any pipeline step has superlinear scaling (the O(n²) deduplication from Module 8 before optimization), the actual growth will be faster than projected. At CelticWind, the capacity projection is run quarterly with the actual growth rate (measured from the last 12 months of rows_processed metrics) and the actual runtime (measured from the pipeline_duration_seconds metric). The projection identifies the year when headroom drops below 20% — the threshold where proactive optimization should begin. For the current pipeline, the 20% threshold is approximately year 8, providing comfortable planning horizon.
Planning Infrastructure Changes

When the capacity projection shows headroom approaching 20%, the team evaluates three categories of response. First, optimize the pipeline: the Module 8 diagnostic workflow identifies the current bottleneck and the potential improvement (can we gain another 2x from algorithmic changes or Polars optimizations?). Second, scale the infrastructure: can the server be upgraded from 4 GB to 8 GB RAM, or from 4 cores to 8 cores (enabling more parallel workers in Module 2)? Third, change the architecture: should the pipeline move from single-server Python to distributed PySpark, which handles data growth by adding cluster nodes rather than optimizing single-node code? At CelticWind, the progression is: optimize first (cheapest), scale second (moderate cost), re-architect last (expensive but necessary at 10x growth).

blocks.title: Capacity planning uses metrics from every previous module
blocks.content: The rows_processed counter (Module 9) provides the data growth rate. The pipeline_duration_seconds gauge (Module 9) provides the runtime trend. The performance baseline (Module 8) identifies which steps scale linearly and which may scale superlinearly. The profiling workflow (Module 8) identifies the bottleneck to optimize. The concurrent pipeline (Module 2) determines how much the infrastructure can be parallelized. The memory budget (Module 3) determines the maximum data volume per chunk. Capacity planning is the synthesis of operational data from every module in the track.

This is the final lesson of the Python for Data Engineers mid-level track. Over 10 modules and 100 lessons, you have transformed CelticWind's pipeline from a junior-level script that worked on one developer's laptop into a production-grade system that 3 teams operate across 12 pipelines with SLA commitments, automated deployment, and 24/7 monitoring. Each module added a layer to the production capability: the architecture that makes pipelines extensible (Module 1), the concurrency that makes them fast (Module 2), the memory efficiency that makes them scalable (Module 3), the framework that eliminates duplication (Module 4), the configuration that makes them portable (Module 5), the testing that proves they work (Module 6), the CI/CD that deploys them safely (Module 7), the profiling that keeps them fast (Module 8), the observability that makes them visible (Module 9), and the operational discipline that keeps them reliable over years (this module).
The Production Playbook

rows: 12 pipelines sharing one framework via protocol-based interfaces
Threading for I/O, multiprocessing for CPU
12 production incidents prevented by quality gates
Tuesday incident: detected in 15 seconds (was 4 hours)

blocks.content: The playbook is not a checklist to implement all at once — it is a reference for which concern to address when the need arises. A team with 3 pipelines needs Modules 1, 6, and 7 (architecture, testing, deployment) before Modules 4 and 5 (framework, configuration). A team hitting memory limits needs Module 3 before Module 8 (memory efficiency before CPU profiling). A team with frequent production incidents needs Modules 9 and 10 (observability, operations) before Module 2 (concurrency). The playbook's value is knowing which module provides which capability — and reaching for the right module when the need emerges.

The CelticWind scenario has grown from 400,000 meters and 1 team (junior track) to 1.2 million meters, 8 sub-regions, 3 teams, and 12 pipelines (mid-level track). The senior track continues the story: CelticWind merges with Scottish and Breton energy companies to form the Atlantic Energy Alliance, scaling to 5 million meters across 3 countries with 25 sub-regions, 10 teams, and 200+ pipelines. The patterns from this track — protocols, frameworks, observability, operational discipline — are the foundation that the senior track builds on. The architecture designed here becomes the platform architecture; the configuration system becomes the multi-tenant configuration; the observability stack becomes the enterprise monitoring infrastructure.

blocks.title: You can now operate what you build
blocks.content: The junior track taught you to build pipelines. This track taught you to operate them: to make them fast, testable, deployable, observable, and reliable. The shift is from 'I can write code that processes data' to 'I can build systems that 3 teams rely on for billing, analytics, and operations — with SLA commitments, automated deployment, and incident response procedures.' The next track (senior) shifts again: from operating systems to designing platforms that other teams build on. But the operational discipline from this track — idempotency, monitoring, runbooks, capacity planning — remains the foundation for every level of engineering maturity.
